For the historical province, see [Province of Quebec (1763–1791)](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"). For other uses, see [Quebec (disambiguation)](/wiki/Quebec_(disambiguation) "Quebec (disambiguation)").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Province of Canada

This article is about the Canadian province. For the province's capital city, see [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"). For the historical province, see [Province of Quebec (1763–1791)](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"). For other uses, see [Quebec (disambiguation)](/wiki/Quebec_(disambiguation) "Quebec (disambiguation)").

**Quebec**[[a]](#cite_note-11) ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Québec*)[[b]](#cite_note-13) is [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")'s [largest province by area](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_provinces_and_territories_by_area "List of Canadian provinces and territories by area").[[c]](#cite_note-14) Located in [Central Canada](/wiki/Central_Canada "Central Canada"), it is the only [Francophone-majority](/wiki/French_in_Canada "French in Canada") province in the country, being home to [Québécois French](/wiki/Quebec_French "Quebec French"). It shares borders with the provinces of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") to the west, [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") to the northeast, [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") to the southeast and a coastal border with the territory of [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut"). In the south, it shares a border with the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[d]](#cite_note-15) Quebec has a population of around eight million, making it Canada's [second-most populous province](/wiki/Population_of_Canada_by_province_and_territory "Population of Canada by province and territory") only behind Ontario.

Between 1534 and 1763, what is now Quebec was the [French colony](/wiki/List_of_French_possessions_and_colonies "List of French possessions and colonies") of [Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") and was the most developed colony in [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"). Following the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), Canada became a [British colony](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_the_British_Empire#List_of_territories_that_were_once_a_part_of_the_British_Empire "Territorial evolution of the British Empire"), first as the [Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)") (1763–1791), then [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada") (1791–1841), and lastly part of the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada") (1841–1867) as a result of the [Lower Canada Rebellion](/wiki/Lower_Canada_Rebellion "Lower Canada Rebellion"). It was [confederated](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") with Ontario, [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and New Brunswick in 1867. [Until the early 1960s](/wiki/Grande_Noirceur "Grande Noirceur"), the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Canada "Catholic Church in Canada") played a large role in the social and cultural institutions in Quebec. However, the [Quiet Revolution](/wiki/Quiet_Revolution "Quiet Revolution") of the 1960s to 1980s increased the role of the Government of Quebec in *[l'État québécois](/wiki/%C3%89tat_qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "État québécois")* (the public authority of Quebec).

The [Government of Quebec](/wiki/Government_of_Quebec "Government of Quebec") functions within the context of a [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") and is both a [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") and a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"). The [Premier of Quebec](/wiki/Premier_of_Quebec "Premier of Quebec") acts as [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). [Independence debates](/wiki/Quebec_sovereignty_movement "Quebec sovereignty movement") have played a large role in [Quebec politics](/wiki/Quebec_politics "Quebec politics"). Quebec society's [cohesion](/wiki/Social_order "Social order") and [specificity](/wiki/Distinct_society "Distinct society") is based on three of its unique [statutory](/wiki/Statutory_law "Statutory law") documents: the *[Quebec Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Quebec_Charter_of_Human_Rights_and_Freedoms "Quebec Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms")*, the *[Charter of the French Language](/wiki/Charter_of_the_French_Language "Charter of the French Language")*, and the *[Civil Code of Quebec](/wiki/Civil_Code_of_Quebec "Civil Code of Quebec")*. Furthermore, unlike elsewhere in Canada which uses a purely [common-law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") system, [law in Quebec](/wiki/Quebec_law "Quebec law") is mixed: [private law](/wiki/Private_law "Private law") is exercised under a [civil-law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") system, while [public law](/wiki/Public_law "Public law") is exercised under a [common-law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") system.

The [economy of Quebec](/wiki/Economy_of_Quebec "Economy of Quebec") is mainly supported by its large service sector and varied industrial sector. For exports, it leans on the key industries of [aeronautics](/wiki/Aeronautics "Aeronautics"), [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydro-Qu%C3%A9bec "Hydro-Québec"), mining, [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry "Pharmaceutical industry"), aluminum, wood, and paper. Quebec is well known for producing [maple syrup](/wiki/Maple_syrup "Maple syrup"), for [its comedy](/wiki/Comedy_in_Quebec "Comedy in Quebec"), and for making [hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") one of the most [popular sports in Canada](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_Canada "Ice hockey in Canada"). It is also renowned its distinct [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Quebec "Culture of Quebec"); the province produces [literature](/wiki/Quebec_literature "Quebec literature"), [music](/wiki/Music_of_Quebec "Music of Quebec"), [films](/wiki/Cinema_of_Quebec "Cinema of Quebec"), [TV shows](/wiki/List_of_French-language_Canadian_television_series "List of French-language Canadian television series"), [festivals](/wiki/List_of_festivals_in_Quebec "List of festivals in Quebec"), and more.

## Etymology

The name *Québec* comes from an [Algonquin](/wiki/Algonquin_language "Algonquin language") word meaning 'narrow passage' or 'strait'.[[12]](#cite_note-16) The name originally referred to the area around [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") where the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") narrows to a cliff-lined gap. Early variations in the spelling included *Québecq* and *Kébec*.[[13]](#cite_note-17) French explorer [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") chose the name *Québec* in 1608 for the colonial outpost he would use as the [administrative seat](/wiki/Administrative_seat "Administrative seat") for [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France").[[14]](#cite_note-18)

## History

Main article: [History of Quebec](/wiki/History_of_Quebec "History of Quebec")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Quebec history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Quebec_history "Timeline of Quebec history").

### Indigenous peoples and European expeditions (pre-1608)

Main articles: [Peopling of the Americas](/wiki/Peopling_of_the_Americas "Peopling of the Americas"), [Indigenous peoples in Quebec](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Quebec "Indigenous peoples in Quebec"), and [Exploration of North America](/wiki/Exploration_of_North_America "Exploration of North America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Jacques_Cartier_1851-1852.jpg/250px-Jacques_Cartier_1851-1852.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacques_Cartier_1851-1852.jpg)

A depiction of [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") by [Théophile Hamel](/wiki/Th%C3%A9ophile_Hamel "Théophile Hamel"), 1844

The [Paleo-Indians](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians"), theorized to have migrated from Asia to America between 20,000 and 14,000 years ago, were the first people to establish themselves on the lands of Quebec, arriving after the [Laurentide Ice Sheet](/wiki/Laurentide_Ice_Sheet "Laurentide Ice Sheet") melted roughly 11,000 years ago.[[15]](#cite_note-19)[[16]](#cite_note-20) From them, many [ethnocultural groups](/wiki/Ethnic_groups "Ethnic groups") emerged. By the European explorations of the 1500s, there were eleven [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Quebec "Indigenous peoples in Quebec"): the [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") and ten [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") – the [Abenaki](/wiki/Abenaki "Abenaki"), [Algonquin](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people") (or Anichinabés), [Atikamekw](/wiki/Atikamekw "Atikamekw"), [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree"), [Huron-Wendat](/wiki/Wendat_people "Wendat people"), [Wolastoqiyik](/wiki/Wolastoqiyik "Wolastoqiyik"), [Miꞌkmaq](/wiki/Mi%EA%9E%8Ckmaq "Miꞌkmaq"), [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), [Innu](/wiki/Innu "Innu") and [Naskapi](/wiki/Naskapi "Naskapi").[[17]](#cite_note-21) Algonquians organized into seven political entities and lived nomadic lives based on hunting, gathering, and fishing.[[18]](#cite_note-22) Inuit fished and hunted whales and seals along the coasts of Hudson and Ungava Bays.[[19]](#cite_note-Marsh19882-23)

In the 15th century, the [Byzantine Empire fell](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople"), prompting Western Europeans to search for new [sea routes](/wiki/Sea_lane "Sea lane") to the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharpentierDurocherLavilleLinteau198547-24) As such, around 1522–23, [Giovanni da Verrazzano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Giovanni da Verrazzano") persuaded [King Francis I of France](/wiki/King_Francis_I_of_France "King Francis I of France") to commission an expedition to find a western route to [Cathay](/wiki/Cathay "Cathay") (China) via a [Northwest Passage](/wiki/Northwest_Passage "Northwest Passage"). Though this expedition was unsuccessful, it established the name *New France* for northeast North America.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharpentierDurocherLavilleLinteau198550-25) In his first expedition ordered from the Kingdom of France, [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") became the first European explorer to discover and map Quebec when he landed in [Gaspé](/wiki/Gasp%C3%A9,_Quebec "Gaspé, Quebec") on July 24, 1534.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiendeau200736-26) In the second expedition, in 1535, Cartier explored the lands of [Stadacona](/wiki/Stadacona "Stadacona") and named the village and its surrounding territories *[Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)")* (from *kanata*, 'village' in [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquoian_language "Iroquoian language")). Cartier returned to France with about 10 [St. Lawrence Iroquoians](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Iroquoians "St. Lawrence Iroquoians"), including Chief [Donnacona](/wiki/Donnacona "Donnacona"). In 1540, Donnacona told the legend of the [Kingdom of Saguenay](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saguenay "Kingdom of Saguenay") to the King, inspiring him to order a third expedition, this time led by [Jean-François de La Rocque de Roberval](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_de_La_Rocque_de_Roberval "Jean-François de La Rocque de Roberval"); it was unsuccessful in its goal of finding the kingdom.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharpentierDurocherLavilleLinteau198551-27)

After these expeditions, France mostly abandoned North America for 50 years because of its financial crisis; France was involved in the [Italian Wars](/wiki/Italian_Wars "Italian Wars") and religious wars.[[24]](#cite_note-28) Around 1580, the rise of the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") reignited French interest; New France became a [colonial trading post](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)").[[25]](#cite_note-mathieu-29) In 1603, [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") travelled to the Saint Lawrence River and, on Pointe Saint-Mathieu, established a [defence pact](/wiki/Defence_pact "Defence pact") with the Innu, Wolastoqiyik and Mi'kmaq, that would be "a decisive factor in the maintenance of a French colonial enterprise in America despite an enormous numerical disadvantage vis-à-vis the British".[[26]](#cite_note-Litalien20042-30) Thus also began French military support to the [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples") and Huron peoples against Iroquois attacks; these became known as the [Iroquois Wars](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars") and lasted from the early 1600s to the early 1700s.[[27]](#cite_note-31)

### New France (1608–1763)

Main articles: [Canada (New France)](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") and [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Three_chiefs_of_the_Huron.jpg/250px-Three_chiefs_of_the_Huron.jpg)](/wiki/File:Three_chiefs_of_the_Huron.jpg)

Three Huron-Wendat chiefs from [Wendake](/wiki/Wendake,_Quebec "Wendake, Quebec"). New France had largely peaceful relations with the Indigenous people, such as their allies the Huron. After the [defeat of the Huron](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars") by their mutual enemy, the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), many fled from Ontario to Quebec.

In 1608, Samuel de Champlain[[28]](#cite_note-32) returned to the region as head of an exploration party. On July 3, 1608, with the support of King [Henry IV](/wiki/Henri_IV "Henri IV"), he founded the [Habitation de Québec](/wiki/Habitation_de_Qu%C3%A9bec "Habitation de Québec") (now Quebec City) and made it the capital of New France and its regions.[[25]](#cite_note-mathieu-29) The settlement was built as a permanent fur trading outpost, where First Nations traded furs for French goods, such as metal objects, guns, alcohol, and clothing.[[29]](#cite_note-LeaMilward20012-33) Missionary groups arrived in New France after the founding of Quebec City. [Coureurs des bois](/wiki/Coureurs_des_bois "Coureurs des bois") and [Catholic missionaries](/wiki/Catholic_missions "Catholic missions") used river [canoes](/wiki/Canoe "Canoe") to explore the interior and establish fur trading forts.[[30]](#cite_note-Poulter20102-34)[[31]](#cite_note-Chartrand2013b2-35)

The [Compagnie des Cent-Associés](/wiki/Compagnie_des_Cent-Associ%C3%A9s "Compagnie des Cent-Associés"), which had been granted a royal mandate to manage New France in 1627, introduced the [Custom of Paris](/wiki/Custom_of_Paris_in_New_France "Custom of Paris in New France") and the [seigneurial system](/wiki/Seigneurial_system_of_New_France "Seigneurial system of New France"), and forbade settlement by anyone other than Catholics.[[32]](#cite_note-Harris19842-36) In 1629, [Quebec City surrendered](/wiki/Surrender_of_Quebec "Surrender of Quebec"), without battle, to English [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") during the [Anglo-French War](/wiki/Anglo-French_War_(1627%E2%80%9329) "Anglo-French War (1627–29)"); in 1632, the English king agreed to return it with the [Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1632) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1632)"). [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res "Trois-Rivières") was founded at de Champlain's request in 1634.[[33]](#cite_note-37) [Paul de Chomedey de Maisonneuve](/wiki/Paul_de_Chomedey,_Sieur_de_Maisonneuve "Paul de Chomedey, Sieur de Maisonneuve") founded [Ville-Marie](/wiki/Fort_Ville-Marie "Fort Ville-Marie") (now Montreal) in 1642.

In 1663, the Company of New France ceded Canada to [King Louis XIV](/wiki/King_Louis_XIV "King Louis XIV"), who made New France into a royal province of France.[[34]](#cite_note-rois-38) New France was now a [true colony](/wiki/First_French_colonial_empire "First French colonial empire") administered by the [Sovereign Council of New France](/wiki/Sovereign_Council_of_New_France "Sovereign Council of New France") from Quebec City. A [governor-general](/wiki/Governor_of_New_France "Governor of New France"), governed Canada and its administrative dependencies: Acadia, [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)") and Plaisance.[[35]](#cite_note-Hayes20082-39) The French settlers were mostly farmers and known as "[Canadiens](/wiki/Canadiens "Canadiens")" or "[Habitants](/wiki/Habitants "Habitants")". Though there was little immigration,[[36]](#cite_note-Preston20092-40) the colony grew because of the Habitants' high birth rates.[[37]](#cite_note-Powell20092-41)[[38]](#cite_note-McIlwraithMuller20012-42) In 1665, the [Carignan-Salières regiment](/wiki/R%C3%A9giment_de_Carignan-Sali%C3%A8res "Régiment de Carignan-Salières") developed the string of fortifications known as the "Valley of Forts" to protect against Iroquois invasions and brought with them 1,200 new men.[[39]](#cite_note-43) To redress the gender imbalance and boost population growth, King Louis XIV sponsored the passage of approximately 800 young French women ([King's Daughters](/wiki/Les_filles_du_roi "Les filles du roi")) to the colony.[[34]](#cite_note-rois-38) In 1666, intendant [Jean Talon](/wiki/Jean_Talon "Jean Talon") organized the first census and counted 3,215 Habitants. Talon enacted policies to diversify agriculture and encourage births, which, in 1672, had increased the population to 6,700.[[40]](#cite_note-44)

New France's territory grew to extend from [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay") to the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"), and would encompass the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes").[[41]](#cite_note-45) In the early 1700s, [Governor Callières](/wiki/Louis-Hector_de_Calli%C3%A8re "Louis-Hector de Callière") concluded the [Great Peace of Montreal](/wiki/Great_Peace_of_Montreal "Great Peace of Montreal"), which not only confirmed the alliance between the Algonquian and New France, but definitively ended the Iroquois Wars.[[42]](#cite_note-46) From 1688 onwards, the fierce competition between the French and British to control North America's interior and monopolize fur trade pitted New France and its Indigenous allies against the Iroquois and English in four successive wars called the [French and Indian Wars](/wiki/French_and_Indian_Wars "French and Indian Wars") by Americans, and the Intercolonial Wars in Quebec.[[43]](#cite_note-47) The first three were [King William's War](/wiki/King_William%27s_War "King William's War") (1688–1697), [Queen Anne's War](/wiki/Queen_Anne%27s_War "Queen Anne's War") (1702–1713), and [King George's War](/wiki/King_George%27s_War "King George's War") (1744–1748). In 1713, following the [Peace of Utrecht](/wiki/Peace_of_Utrecht "Peace of Utrecht"), the [Duke of Orléans](/wiki/Philippe_II,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Philippe II, Duke of Orléans") ceded Acadia and [Plaisance Bay](/wiki/Placentia_Bay "Placentia Bay") to Great Britain, but retained [Île Saint-Jean](/wiki/Isle_Saint-Jean "Isle Saint-Jean"), and [Île-Royale](/wiki/%C3%8Ele-Royale_(New_France) "Île-Royale (New France)") where the [Fortress of Louisbourg](/wiki/Fortress_of_Louisbourg "Fortress of Louisbourg") was subsequently erected. These losses were significant since Plaisance Bay was the primary communication route between New France and France, and Acadia contained 5,000 [Acadians](/wiki/Acadian_people "Acadian people").[[44]](#cite_note-48)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharpentierDurocherLavilleLinteau1985115-49) In the [siege of Louisbourg (1745)](/wiki/Siege_of_Louisbourg_(1745) "Siege of Louisbourg (1745)"), the British were victorious, but returned the city to France after war concessions.[[46]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Montcalm_leading_his_troops_at_the_Plains_of_Abraham.jpg/250px-Montcalm_leading_his_troops_at_the_Plains_of_Abraham.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montcalm_leading_his_troops_at_the_Plains_of_Abraham.jpg)

Montcalm leading his troops into battle. Watercolour by [Charles William Jefferys](/wiki/Charles_William_Jefferys "Charles William Jefferys").

The last of the four French and Indian Wars was the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") ("The War of the [Conquest](/wiki/Conquest_of_New_France "Conquest of New France")" in Quebec) and lasted from 1754 to 1763.[[47]](#cite_note-51)[[48]](#cite_note-52) In 1754, tensions escalated for control of the [Ohio Valley](/wiki/Ohio_Valley "Ohio Valley"), as authorities in New France became more aggressive in efforts to expel British traders and colonists.[[49]](#cite_note-53) In 1754, [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") launched a surprise attack on a group of sleeping Canadien soldiers, known as the [Battle of Jumonville Glen](/wiki/Battle_of_Jumonville_Glen "Battle of Jumonville Glen"), the first battle of the war. In 1755, Governor [Charles Lawrence](/wiki/Charles_Lawrence_(British_Army_officer) "Charles Lawrence (British Army officer)") and Officer [Robert Monckton](/wiki/Robert_Monckton "Robert Monckton") ordered the forceful [expulsion of the Acadians](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Acadians "Expulsion of the Acadians"). In 1758, on Île-Royale, British General James Wolfe [besieged and captured](/wiki/Siege_of_Louisbourg_(1758) "Siege of Louisbourg (1758)") the Fortress of Louisbourg.[[50]](#cite_note-54) This allowed him to control access to the [Gulf of St. Lawrence](/wiki/Gulf_of_St._Lawrence "Gulf of St. Lawrence") through the [Cabot Strait](/wiki/Cabot_Strait "Cabot Strait"). In 1759, he besieged Quebec for three months from [Île d'Orléans](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_d%27Orl%C3%A9ans "Île d'Orléans").[[51]](#cite_note-55) Then, Wolfe stormed Quebec and fought against [Montcalm](/wiki/Louis-Joseph_de_Montcalm "Louis-Joseph de Montcalm") for control of the city in the [Battle of the Plains of Abraham](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Plains_of_Abraham "Battle of the Plains of Abraham"). After a British victory, the [king's lieutenant](/wiki/Steward_(office) "Steward (office)") and [Lord of Ramezay](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Nicolas_Roch_de_Ramezay "Jean-Baptiste Nicolas Roch de Ramezay") concluded the [Articles of Capitulation of Quebec](/wiki/Articles_of_Capitulation_of_Quebec "Articles of Capitulation of Quebec"). During the spring of 1760, the [Chevalier de Lévis](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Gaston_de_L%C3%A9vis "François Gaston de Lévis") besieged Quebec City and forced the British to entrench themselves during the [Battle of Sainte-Foy](/wiki/Battle_of_Sainte-Foy "Battle of Sainte-Foy"). However, loss of French vessels sent to resupply New France after the fall of Quebec City during the [Battle of Restigouche](/wiki/Battle_of_Restigouche "Battle of Restigouche") marked the end of France's efforts to retake the colony. [Governor Pierre de Rigaud, marquis de Vaudreuil-Cavagnial](/wiki/Pierre_de_Rigaud,_marquis_de_Vaudreuil-Cavagnial "Pierre de Rigaud, marquis de Vaudreuil-Cavagnial") signed the [Articles of Capitulation of Montreal](/wiki/Articles_of_Capitulation_of_Montreal "Articles of Capitulation of Montreal") on September 8, 1760.

While awaiting the results of the Seven Years' War in Europe, New France was put under a [British military regime](/wiki/British_military_regime_in_New_France "British military regime in New France") led by Governor [James Murray](/wiki/James_Murray_(British_Army_officer,_born_1721) "James Murray (British Army officer, born 1721)").[[52]](#cite_note-britannica_murray-56) In 1762, Commander [Jeffery Amherst](/wiki/Jeffery_Amherst "Jeffery Amherst") ended the French presence in Newfoundland at the [Battle of Signal Hill](/wiki/Battle_of_Signal_Hill "Battle of Signal Hill"). France secretly ceded the western part of Louisiana and the [Mississippi River Delta](/wiki/Mississippi_River_Delta "Mississippi River Delta") to Spain via the [Treaty of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fontainebleau_(1762) "Treaty of Fontainebleau (1762)"). On February 10, 1763, the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") concluded the war. France ceded its North American possessions to Great Britain.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter1999505–506-57) Thus, France had put an end to New France and abandoned the remaining 60,000 Canadiens, who sided with the Catholic [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") in refusing to take an oath to the [British Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown").[[54]](#cite_note-58) The rupture from France would provoke a transformation within the [descendants of the Canadiens](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") that would eventually result in the birth of a new [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation").[[55]](#cite_note-59)

### British North America (1763–1867)

Main articles: [Province of Quebec (1763–1791)](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"), [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada"), and [Canada East](/wiki/Canada_East "Canada East")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Province_of_Quebec_1774.gif/250px-Province_of_Quebec_1774.gif)](/wiki/File:Province_of_Quebec_1774.gif)

The [Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763-1791) "Province of Quebec (1763-1791)") in 1774

After the British acquired Canada in 1763, the British government established a constitution for the newly acquired territory, under the [Royal Proclamation](/wiki/Royal_Proclamation_of_1763 "Royal Proclamation of 1763").[[56]](#cite_note-60) The Canadiens were subordinated to the government of the [British Empire](/wiki/First_British_Empire "First British Empire") and circumscribed to a region of the [St. Lawrence Valley](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River") and [Anticosti Island](/wiki/Anticosti_Island "Anticosti Island") called the [Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"). With unrest growing in their southern colonies, the British were worried that the Canadiens might support what would become the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). To secure allegiance to the British crown, Governor [James Murray](/wiki/James_Murray_(Quebec_governor) "James Murray (Quebec governor)") and later Governor [Guy Carleton](/wiki/Guy_Carleton,_1st_Baron_Dorchester "Guy Carleton, 1st Baron Dorchester") promoted the need for accommodations, resulting in the enactment of the [Quebec Act](/wiki/Quebec_Act "Quebec Act")[[57]](#cite_note-61) of 1774. This act allowed Canadiens to regain their [civil customs](/wiki/Law_in_Quebec "Law in Quebec"), return to the seigneural system, regain certain rights including use of French, and reappropriate their old territories: Labrador, the Great Lakes, the Ohio Valley, [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country") and the [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory").[[58]](#cite_note-Geographers19682-62)

As early as 1774, the [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress") of the separatist [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") attempted to rally the Canadiens to its cause. However, [its military troops](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") failed to defeat the British counteroffensive during its [Invasion of Quebec](/wiki/Invasion_of_Quebec_(1775) "Invasion of Quebec (1775)") in 1775. Most Canadiens remained neutral, though some regiments allied themselves with the Americans in the [Saratoga campaign](/wiki/Saratoga_campaign "Saratoga campaign") of 1777. When the British recognized the independence of the rebel colonies at the signing of the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") of 1783, it ceded Illinois and the Ohio Valley to the newly formed United States and denoted the 45th parallel as its border, drastically reducing Quebec's size.

Some [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalists "United Empire Loyalists") from the US migrated to Quebec and populated various regions.[[59]](#cite_note-63) Dissatisfied with the legal rights under the French seigneurial regime which applied in Quebec, and wanting to use the British legal system to which they were accustomed, the Loyalists protested to British authorities until the [Constitutional Act](/wiki/Constitutional_Act_1791 "Constitutional Act 1791") of 1791 was enacted, dividing the Province of Quebec into two distinct colonies starting from the [Ottawa River](/wiki/Ottawa_River "Ottawa River"): [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") to the west (predominantly Anglo-Protestant) and [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada") to the east (Franco-Catholic). Lower Canada's lands consisted of the coasts of the Saint Lawrence River, Labrador and Anticosti Island, with the territory extending north to [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land"), and south, east and west to the borders with the US, New Brunswick, and Upper Canada. The creation of Upper and Lower Canada allowed Loyalists to live under British laws and institutions, while Canadiens could maintain their French civil law and Catholic religion. Governor Haldimand drew Loyalists away from Quebec City and Montreal by offering free land on the north shore of Lake Ontario to anyone willing to swear allegiance to George III. During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), [Charles-Michel de Salaberry](/wiki/Charles-Michel_de_Salaberry "Charles-Michel de Salaberry") became a hero by leading the Canadian troops to victory at the [Battle of the Chateauguay](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Chateauguay "Battle of the Chateauguay"). This loss caused the Americans to abandon the Saint Lawrence Campaign, their major strategic effort to conquer Canada.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Saint-Eustache-Patriotes.jpg/250px-Saint-Eustache-Patriotes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint-Eustache-Patriotes.jpg)

The [Battle of Saint-Eustache](/wiki/Battle_of_Saint-Eustache "Battle of Saint-Eustache") was the final battle of the Lower Canada Rebellion.[[60]](#cite_note-rebellion-64)

Gradually, the [Legislative Assembly of Lower Canada](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Lower_Canada "Legislative Assembly of Lower Canada"), who represented the people, came into conflict with the superior authority of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") and [its appointed representatives](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Lower_Canada "Legislative Council of Lower Canada"). Starting in 1791, the government of Lower Canada was criticized and contested by the [Parti canadien](/wiki/Parti_canadien "Parti canadien"). In 1834, the Parti canadien presented its [92 resolutions](/wiki/Ninety-Two_Resolutions "Ninety-Two Resolutions"), political demands which expressed loss of confidence in the [British monarchy](/wiki/British_monarchy "British monarchy"). Discontentment intensified throughout the [public meetings](/wiki/List_of_the_public_meetings_held_in_Lower_Canada_between_May_and_November_1837 "List of the public meetings held in Lower Canada between May and November 1837") of 1837, and the [Lower Canada Rebellion](/wiki/Lower_Canada_Rebellion "Lower Canada Rebellion") began in 1837.[[61]](#cite_note-65) In 1837, [Louis-Joseph Papineau](/wiki/Louis-Joseph_Papineau "Louis-Joseph Papineau") and [Robert Nelson](/wiki/Robert_Nelson_(insurrectionist) "Robert Nelson (insurrectionist)") led residents of Lower Canada to form an armed group called the [Patriotes](/wiki/Patriotes "Patriotes"). [They declared independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Lower_Canada "Declaration of Independence of Lower Canada") in 1838, guaranteeing rights and equality for all citizens without discrimination.[[62]](#cite_note-66) Their actions resulted in rebellions in both Lower and [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada_Rebellion "Upper Canada Rebellion"). The Patriotes were victorious in their first battle, the [Battle of Saint-Denis](/wiki/Battle_of_Saint-Denis_(1837) "Battle of Saint-Denis (1837)"). However, they were unorganized and badly equipped, leading to their loss against the British army in the [Battle of Saint-Charles](/wiki/Battle_of_Saint-Charles "Battle of Saint-Charles"), and defeat in the [Battle of Saint-Eustache](/wiki/Battle_of_Saint-Eustache "Battle of Saint-Eustache").[[60]](#cite_note-rebellion-64)

In response to the rebellions, [Lord Durham](/wiki/John_Lambton,_1st_Earl_of_Durham "John Lambton, 1st Earl of Durham") was asked to undertake a study and prepare a [report offering a solution](/wiki/Report_on_the_Affairs_of_British_North_America "Report on the Affairs of British North America") to the British Parliament.[[63]](#cite_note-durham2-67) Durham recommended that Canadiens be [culturally assimilated](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation"), with English as their only official language. To do this, the British passed the *[Act of Union 1840](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1840 "Act of Union 1840")*, which merged Upper Canada and Lower Canada into a single colony: the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"). Lower Canada became the francophone and densely populated [Canada East](/wiki/Canada_East "Canada East"), and Upper Canada became the anglophone and sparsely populated [Canada West](/wiki/Canada_West "Canada West"). This union, unsurprisingly, was the main source of political instability until 1867. Despite their population gap, Canada East and Canada West obtained an identical number of seats in the [Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_the_Province_of_Canada "Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada"), which created representation problems. In the beginning, Canada East was underrepresented because of its superior population size. Over time, however, massive immigration from the British Isles to Canada West occurred. Since the two regions continued to have equal representation, this meant it was now Canada West that was under-represented. The representation issues were called into question by debates on "[representation by population](/wiki/Representation_(politics) "Representation (politics)")". Around this period, the [British population](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians") appropriated the term *Canadian* to refer to themselves, referring to Canada, their place of residence. The French population, who had thus far been "the Canadians", began to be identified with their [ethnic community](/wiki/Ethnic_community "Ethnic community") under the name "[French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian")" as they were a "French of Canada".[[64]](#cite_note-68)

As access to new lands remained problematic because they were still monopolized by the [Château Clique](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_Clique "Château Clique"), an exodus of Canadiens towards [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") began and went on for the next hundred years. This phenomenon is known as the [Grande Hémorragie](/wiki/Grande_H%C3%A9morragie "Grande Hémorragie") and threatened the survival of the Canadien nation. The massive British immigration ordered from London that followed the failed rebellion, compounded this. To combat it, the Church adopted the [revenge of the cradle](/wiki/La_Revanche_des_berceaux "La Revanche des berceaux") policy. In 1844, the capital of the Province of Canada was moved from [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario") to Montreal.[[65]](#cite_note-69) During Ireland's [Great Potato Famine](/wiki/Great_Potato_Famine "Great Potato Famine") (1845–1852), nearly 100,000 Irish refugees passed through [Grosse Isle](/wiki/Grosse_Isle "Grosse Isle")'s quarantine station, with many settling in Quebec and integrating into French-Canadian society.[[66]](#cite_note-70)[[67]](#cite_note-71)

Political unrest came to a head in 1849, when English Canadian rioters [set fire to the Parliament Building in Montreal](/wiki/Burning_of_the_Parliament_Buildings_in_Montreal "Burning of the Parliament Buildings in Montreal") following the enactment of the *[Rebellion Losses Bill](/wiki/Rebellion_Losses_Bill "Rebellion Losses Bill")*, a law that compensated French Canadians whose properties were destroyed during the rebellions of 1837–1838.[[68]](#cite_note-72) This bill, resulting from the [Baldwin](/wiki/Robert_Baldwin "Robert Baldwin")-[La Fontaine](/wiki/Louis-Hippolyte_Lafontaine "Louis-Hippolyte Lafontaine") coalition and Lord Elgin's advice, was important as it established the notion of [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government").[[69]](#cite_note-73) In 1854, the seigneurial system was abolished, the [Grand Trunk Railway](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Railway "Grand Trunk Railway") was built, and the [Canadian–American Reciprocity Treaty](/wiki/Canadian%E2%80%93American_Reciprocity_Treaty "Canadian–American Reciprocity Treaty") was implemented. In 1866, the *[Civil Code of Lower Canada](/wiki/Civil_Code_of_Lower_Canada "Civil Code of Lower Canada")* was adopted.[[70]](#cite_note-74)[[71]](#cite_note-75)[[72]](#cite_note-76)

### Traditional Quebec (1867–1960)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/George-Etienne_Cartier.jpg/250px-George-Etienne_Cartier.jpg)](/wiki/File:George-Etienne_Cartier.jpg)

[George-Étienne Cartier](/wiki/George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier "George-Étienne Cartier"), co-premier from [Canada East](/wiki/Canada_East "Canada East") and a Father of Confederation

In 1864, negotiations began for [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") between the Province of Canada, New Brunswick and Nova Scotia at the [Charlottetown Conference](/wiki/Charlottetown_Conference "Charlottetown Conference") and [Quebec Conference](/wiki/Quebec_Conference,_1864 "Quebec Conference, 1864").

After having fought as a Patriote, [George-Étienne Cartier](/wiki/George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier "George-Étienne Cartier") entered politics in the Province of Canada, becoming one of the co-premiers and advocate for the union of the British North American provinces. He became a leading figure at the Quebec Conference, which produced the [Quebec Resolutions](/wiki/Quebec_Resolutions "Quebec Resolutions"), the foundation for Canadian Confederation.[[73]](#cite_note-77) Recognized as a [Father of Confederation](/wiki/Father_of_Confederation "Father of Confederation"), he successfully argued for the establishment of the province of Quebec, initially composed of the historic heart of the territory of the French Canadian nation and where French Canadians would most likely retain majority status.

Following the [London Conference of 1866](/wiki/London_Conference_of_1866 "London Conference of 1866"), the Quebec Resolutions were implemented as the *[British North America Act, 1867](/wiki/British_North_America_Act,_1867 "British North America Act, 1867")*, and brought into force on July 1, 1867, creating Canada, composed of four founding provinces: New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and Quebec. These last two came from splitting the Province of Canada, and used the old borders of Lower Canada for Quebec, and Upper Canada for Ontario. On July 15, 1867, [Pierre-Joseph-Olivier Chauveau](/wiki/Pierre-Joseph-Olivier_Chauveau "Pierre-Joseph-Olivier Chauveau") became Quebec's [first premier](/wiki/List_of_premiers_of_Quebec "List of premiers of Quebec").

Between the late 19th and the late 20th century, Montreal was Canada's and Quebec's most populous city, including its economic and cultural centres. It was, as such, often among the first to adopt new technologies. It launched Canada's first public transit system in 1861 with horse-drawn streetcars,[[74]](#cite_note-78) started a telephone service in 1878,[[75]](#cite_note-79) and received electricity in 1885.[[76]](#cite_note-80)

The new Dominion quickly became interested in [expansionism](/wiki/Expansionism "Expansionism"), especially westward, purchasing [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land") from the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") in 1870. In 1885, it fought against the francophone Métis in the [North-West Rebellion](/wiki/North-West_Rebellion "North-West Rebellion"), and did not grant [clemency](/wiki/Royal_prerogative_of_mercy#Canada "Royal prerogative of mercy") to [Louis Riel](/wiki/Louis_Riel "Louis Riel"), their leader, after he was sentenced to death. This caused several Quebec liberal and conservative MLAs to form the [Parti National](/wiki/Parti_National "Parti National") out of anger.[[77]](#cite_note-81) This, in combination with the [Manitoba Schools Question](/wiki/Manitoba_Schools_Question "Manitoba Schools Question"), also helped turn the promotion and defence of the rights of French Canadians into an important concern. Gradually gaining in popularity, clerico-nationalists – who promoted the Triple Ideal of Catholicism, French, and rural life, alongside other traditional values (e.g., traditional gender roles, resistance to cultural assimilation, anti-[progressivism](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism"), hierarchy) – went on to wield significant influence until the 1960s.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

Montreal continued its expansions into new advances by introducing streetcars in 1892[[79]](#cite_note-83) and seeing bikes and automobiles populate its roads by the 1890s and 1900s respectively.[[80]](#cite_note-84) The Canadian Parliament, meanwhile, expanded Quebec in 1898 by enacting the *[Quebec Boundary Extension Act, 1898](/wiki/Quebec_Boundary_Extension_Act,_1898 "Quebec Boundary Extension Act, 1898")*, which gave Quebec part of Rupert's Land.[[81]](#cite_note-85)

Under the aegis of the Catholic Church and the political action of [Henri Bourassa](/wiki/Henri_Bourassa "Henri Bourassa"), symbols of French Canadian national pride were developed, like the [Flag of Carillon](/wiki/Flag_of_Carillon "Flag of Carillon"), and "[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")" – a patriotic song composed for [Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day](/wiki/Saint-Jean-Baptiste_Day "Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day"). Many organizations went on to consecrate the affirmation of the French-Canadian people, including the [caisses populaires Desjardins](/wiki/Caisses_populaires_Desjardins "Caisses populaires Desjardins") in 1900, the [Club de hockey Canadien](/wiki/Club_de_hockey_Canadien "Club de hockey Canadien") in 1909, *[Le Devoir](/wiki/Le_Devoir "Le Devoir")* in 1910, the [Congress on the French language in Canada](/wiki/Congress_on_the_French_language_in_Canada "Congress on the French language in Canada") in 1912, and *[L'Action nationale](/wiki/L%27Action_nationale "L'Action nationale")* in 1917. In 1909, the Quebec government passed a law obligating wood and pulp to be transformed in Quebec, which helped slow the Grande Hémorragie by allowing Quebec to export its finished products to the US instead of its labourers.[[82]](#cite_note-86) In 1910, [Armand Lavergne](/wiki/Armand_Lavergne "Armand Lavergne") passed the [Lavergne Law](/wiki/Lavergne_Law "Lavergne Law"), the first language legislation in Quebec, which required the use of French alongside English on tickets, documents, bills and contracts issued by transportation and public utility companies. At this time, companies rarely recognized the majority language of Quebec.[[83]](#cite_note-87) This movement may be what ensured Ontario's [Regulation 17](/wiki/Regulation_17 "Regulation 17") (1912–1927) was fought against until its repeal.

In 1912, the Canadian Parliament enacted the *[Quebec Boundaries Extension Act, 1912](/wiki/Quebec_Boundaries_Extension_Act,_1912 "Quebec Boundaries Extension Act, 1912")*, which gave Quebec its final extension: another part of Rupert's Land called the [District of Ungava](/wiki/District_of_Ungava "District of Ungava").[[84]](#cite_note-Morantz20022-88) Quebec's borders now met the [Hudson Strait](/wiki/Hudson_Strait "Hudson Strait") and vaguely interlapped with [Labrador](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland")'s.

When the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") broke out in 1914, Canada was automatically involved and many English Canadians volunteered. However, because they did not feel the same connection to the British Empire and there was no direct threat to Canada, most French Canadians saw no reason to fight. By late 1916, casualties and waning numbers of volunteers were beginning to cause reinforcement problems. After enormous difficulty in the federal government, because almost every French-speaking MP opposed conscription while almost all English-speaking MPs supported it, the *[Military Service Act, 1917](/wiki/Military_Service_Act,_1917 "Military Service Act, 1917")* became law on August 29, 1917.[[85]](#cite_note-Conscription_au_Canada-89) French Canadians protested in the [Conscription Crisis of 1917](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1917 "Conscription Crisis of 1917"), which led to the [Quebec riot](/w/index.php?title=Quebec_riot&action=edit&redlink=1 "Quebec riot (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89meute_de_Qu%C3%A9bec_de_1918 "fr:Émeute de Québec de 1918")].[[86]](#cite_note-90)

In 1919, the [prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition "Prohibition") of [spirits](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor") was enacted following a [provincial referendum](/wiki/1919_Quebec_prohibition_referendum "1919 Quebec prohibition referendum").[[87]](#cite_note-91) In 1920, Montreal hosted Canada’s first public radio broadcast.[[88]](#cite_note-92) Then, in 1921, prohibition was abolished by the *Alcoholic Beverages Act,* which created the [SAQ](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_des_alcools_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Société des alcools du Québec") and allowed the government to control the sale of alcohol.[[89]](#cite_note-93) This resulted in Quebec having the shortest and lightest prohibition in North America, as well as reaping huge profits from the sale of booze to tourists.[[90]](#cite_note-94) Since the location of the border between Canada and Labrador had never been clear, in 1927, the British [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") gathered to draw one. However, the Quebec government did not recognize the ruling, resulting in a [boundary dispute](/wiki/Labrador_boundary_dispute "Labrador boundary dispute") which [remains ongoing](/wiki/Border_between_Quebec_and_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Border between Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador").

In 1931, the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") was enacted, which confirmed the autonomy of the [Dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") – including Canada and its provinces – from the UK, as well as their free association in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").[[91]](#cite_note-95) In the 1930s, Quebec's economy was affected by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") because it greatly reduced US demand for Quebec exports. Between 1929 and 1932, the unemployment rate increased from 8% to 26%. In an attempt to remedy this, the Quebec government enacted infrastructure projects, campaigns to colonize distant regions, financial assistance to farmers, and the *secours directs* – the ancestor to Canada's [Employment Insurance](/wiki/Employment_Insurance "Employment Insurance").[[92]](#cite_note-96) The poor work opportunities in the US also finally ended the Grande Hémorragie.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Maurice_Duplessis.jpg/250px-Maurice_Duplessis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maurice_Duplessis.jpg)

[Maurice Duplessis](/wiki/Maurice_Duplessis "Maurice Duplessis"), premier of Quebec from 1936 to 1939 and during the *[Grande Noirceur](/wiki/Grande_Noirceur "Grande Noirceur")*. He passed in 1959.

French Canadians remained opposed to conscription during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). When Canada declared war in September 1939, the federal government pledged not to conscript soldiers for overseas service. As the war went on, more and more English Canadians voiced support for conscription, despite firm opposition from French Canada. Following a 1942 poll that showed 73% of Quebec's residents were *against* conscription, while 80% or more were *for* conscription in every other province, the federal government passed Bill 80, which allowed conscription for overseas service. In the [Conscription Crisis of 1944](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1944 "Conscription Crisis of 1944") the [Bloc Populaire](/wiki/Bloc_Populaire "Bloc Populaire") emerged to fight conscription.[[85]](#cite_note-Conscription_au_Canada-89) The stark differences between the values of French and English Canada popularized the expression the "[Two Solitudes](/wiki/Two_Solitudes_(Canadian_society) "Two Solitudes (Canadian society)")".

In the wake of the conscription crisis, [Maurice Duplessis](/wiki/Maurice_Duplessis "Maurice Duplessis") of the [Union Nationale](/wiki/Union_Nationale_(Quebec) "Union Nationale (Quebec)") rose to power once more. His government emphasized clerico-nationalist values and implemented conservative policies now known as the [Grande Noirceur](/wiki/Grande_Noirceur "Grande Noirceur"). These included defending [provincial autonomy](/wiki/Autonomism_in_Quebec "Autonomism in Quebec"), promoting Quebec's Catholic and francophone heritage, and favouring [laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire") capitalism over the emerging [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state").[[93]](#cite_note-97) However, with accelerating major changes such as the appearance of television, [baby boom](/wiki/Mid-20th_century_baby_boom "Mid-20th century baby boom"), [workers' conflicts](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action"), electrification of the countryside, emergence of a [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class"), [rural exodus](/wiki/Rural_exodus "Rural exodus") and [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"), expansion of universities and bureaucracies, creation of [motorways](/wiki/Autoroutes_of_Quebec "Autoroutes of Quebec"), and renaissance of literature and poetry, French Canadian society began to develop new ideologies and aspirations.

### Modern Quebec (1960–present)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Maitres_chez_nous_1962.jpg/330px-Maitres_chez_nous_1962.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maitres_chez_nous_1962.jpg)

"*Maîtres chez nous*" was the electoral slogan of the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Quebec_Liberal_Party "Quebec Liberal Party") during the [1962 election](/wiki/1962_Quebec_general_election "1962 Quebec general election").

The [Quiet Revolution](/wiki/Quiet_Revolution "Quiet Revolution") was an intense period of modernization, secularization and social reform, where French Canadians strongly expressed their concern and dissatisfaction with their inferior [socioeconomic position](/wiki/Socioeconomic_status "Socioeconomic status"), and the cultural assimilation of francophone minorities in the English-majority provinces. It resulted in the formation of the modern Québécois identity and [Quebec nationalism](/wiki/Quebec_nationalism "Quebec nationalism").[[94]](#cite_note-98)[[95]](#cite_note-short_history2-99) In 1960, [Jean Lesage](/wiki/Jean_Lesage "Jean Lesage")'s Liberal Party was brought to power with a two-seat majority, having campaigned with the slogan "It's time for things to change".

This government fundamentally restructured Quebec's institutions, creating a modern welfare state through new ministries for education, social affairs, and economic development. It created the [CDPQ](/wiki/Caisse_de_d%C3%A9p%C3%B4t_et_placement_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec"), [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_and_Higher_Education_(Quebec) "Ministry of Education and Higher Education (Quebec)"), [OQLF](/wiki/Office_qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois_de_la_langue_fran%C3%A7aise "Office québécois de la langue française"), Régie des rentes and [Société générale de financement](/wiki/Investissement_Qu%C3%A9bec "Investissement Québec"), and modernized the Labour Code and [Ministry of Social Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_and_Social_Services_(Quebec) "Ministry of Health and Social Services (Quebec)"). In 1962, the government dismantled the financial syndicates of Montreal's [Saint Jacques Street](/wiki/Saint_Jacques_Street "Saint Jacques Street") to weaken the grip of the English-Canadian traditional economic elites. Also in 1962, Natural Resources Minister [René Lévesque](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_L%C3%A9vesque "René Lévesque") led the nationalization of Quebec's private electricity companies to [create a unified Hydro-Québec](/wiki/History_of_Hydro-Qu%C3%A9bec#1963:_Second_stage_of_state_control "History of Hydro-Québec"). This massive project was estimated at over $600 million for the acquisition of eleven companies.[[96]](#cite_note-100)[[97]](#cite_note-101)

The Quiet Revolution was particularly characterized by the 1962 Liberal Party's slogan "Masters in our own house", which, to the Anglo-American conglomerates that dominated the economy and natural resources, announced a collective will for freedom of the French-Canadian people.[[98]](#cite_note-102) As a result of confrontations between the lower [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") and the [laity](/wiki/Laity "Laity"), state institutions began to deliver services without the assistance of the church, and many parts of [civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society") began to be more secular. In 1965, the [Royal Commission on Bilingualism and Biculturalism](/wiki/Royal_Commission_on_Bilingualism_and_Biculturalism "Royal Commission on Bilingualism and Biculturalism")[[99]](#cite_note-103) wrote a preliminary report underlining Quebec's distinct character, and promoted open federalism, a political attitude guaranteeing Quebec a minimum amount of consideration.[[100]](#cite_note-104)[[101]](#cite_note-105) To favour Quebec during its Quiet Revolution, [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson") adopted a policy of open federalism.[[102]](#cite_note-106)[[103]](#cite_note-107) In 1966, the Union Nationale was re-elected and continued on with major reforms.[[104]](#cite_note-108)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/De_Gaule_13a_19670624_3543_2383.jpg/250px-De_Gaule_13a_19670624_3543_2383.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_Gaule_13a_19670624_3543_2383.jpg)

[Charles De Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle"), (1890–1970), on the occasion of [Expo 1967](/wiki/Expo_67 "Expo 67"), [Chemin du Roy](/wiki/Chemin_du_Roy "Chemin du Roy"), [Sainte-Anne-de-la-Pérade](/wiki/Sainte-Anne-de-la-P%C3%A9rade "Sainte-Anne-de-la-Pérade")

In 1967, René Lévesque introduced the concept of *sovereignty-association* in his manifesto [Option Quebec](/wiki/An_Option_for_Quebec "An Option for Quebec"), proposing political independence with economic partnership including a common currency, free trade, and joint institutions. It sparked a constitutional debate on the political future of the province by pitting [federalist](/wiki/Federalism_in_Quebec "Federalism in Quebec") and [sovereignist](/wiki/Quebec_sovereignty_movement "Quebec sovereignty movement") doctrines against each other. The meetings of the [Estates General of French Canada](/wiki/Estates_General_of_French_Canada "Estates General of French Canada") in 1967 marked a tipping point where relations between Quebec and other francophones of Canada ruptured. This deeply affected both parties by fracturing the pan-Canadian French-Canadian identity that had existed before then into: Quebec nationalism, and several minority francophone groups elsewhere.[[105]](#cite_note-109) Also in 1967, President of France [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle") visited Quebec, to attend [Expo 67](/wiki/Expo_67 "Expo 67"). There, he addressed a crowd of more than 100,000, making a speech ending with the exclamation: "Long live free Quebec". This declaration had a profound effect on Quebec by bolstering the burgeoning modern [Quebec sovereignty movement](/wiki/Quebec_sovereignty_movement "Quebec sovereignty movement") and resulting in a diplomatic crisis between France and Canada. Following this, various civilian groups developed, sometimes confronting public authority, for example in the [October Crisis](/wiki/October_Crisis "October Crisis") of 1970.[[106]](#cite_note-Tetley2006AppendixD2-110)

In 1968, [class conflicts](/wiki/Class_conflict "Class conflict") and changes in mentalities intensified.[[107]](#cite_note-111) Quebec artists also started celebrating their distinct identity: [Michel Tremblay](/wiki/Michel_Tremblay "Michel Tremblay")'s 1968 play *Les Belles-sœurs* legitimized [joual](/wiki/Joual "Joual") (working-class Quebec French) as a literary language,[[108]](#cite_note-112) singer-songwriters like [Félix Leclerc](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Leclerc "Félix Leclerc") and [Gilles Vigneault](/wiki/Gilles_Vigneault "Gilles Vigneault") started a new style of Quebec popular music,[[109]](#cite_note-113) and many [local films](/wiki/Cinema_of_Quebec "Cinema of Quebec") began to be produced.[[110]](#cite_note-114) In 1969, the federal [Official Languages Act](/wiki/Official_Languages_Act_(Canada) "Official Languages Act (Canada)") was passed to introduce a linguistic context conducive to Quebec's development.[[111]](#cite_note-115)[[112]](#cite_note-116) In 1973, the liberal government of [Robert Bourassa](/wiki/Robert_Bourassa "Robert Bourassa") initiated the [James Bay Project](/wiki/James_Bay_Project "James Bay Project") on [La Grande River](/wiki/La_Grande_River "La Grande River"). In 1974, it enacted the [Official Language Act](/wiki/Official_Language_Act_(Quebec) "Official Language Act (Quebec)"), which made French the official language of Quebec. In 1975, it established the [Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Quebec_Charter_of_Human_Rights_and_Freedoms "Quebec Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms") and the [James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement](/wiki/James_Bay_and_Northern_Quebec_Agreement "James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Ren%C3%A9_L%C3%A9vesque_BAnQ_%28restored%29.jpg/250px-Ren%C3%A9_L%C3%A9vesque_BAnQ_%28restored%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ren%C3%A9_L%C3%A9vesque_BAnQ_(restored).jpg)

[René Lévesque](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_L%C3%A9vesque "René Lévesque") (1922–1987), one of the architects of the Quiet Revolution, and the Premier of Quebec's first modern sovereignist government

Quebec's first modern sovereignist government, led by [René Lévesque](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_L%C3%A9vesque "René Lévesque"), materialized when the [Parti Québécois](/wiki/Parti_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Parti Québécois") was brought to power in the [1976 Quebec general election](/wiki/1976_Quebec_general_election "1976 Quebec general election").[[113]](#cite_note-117) The [Charter of the French Language](/wiki/Charter_of_the_French_Language "Charter of the French Language") came into force the following year, which increased the use of French. Between 1966 and 1969, the Estates General of French Canada confirmed the [state of Quebec](/wiki/State_of_Quebec "State of Quebec") to be the [nation's fundamental political milieu](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state") and for it to have the right to [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination").[[114]](#cite_note-118)[[115]](#cite_note-119)

In the [1980 referendum](/wiki/1980_Quebec_referendum "1980 Quebec referendum") on sovereignty-association, 40% were for and 60% were against.[[116]](#cite_note-120) After the referendum, Lévesque went back to Ottawa to continue negotiating constitutional changes. On November 4, 1981, the [Kitchen Accord](/wiki/Kitchen_Accord "Kitchen Accord") took place. Delegations from the other nine provinces and the federal government reached an agreement in the absence of Quebec's delegation, which had left for the night.[[117]](#cite_note-121) Because of this, the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Quebec "National Assembly of Quebec") refused to recognize the new [Constitution Act, 1982](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982"), which patriated the Canadian constitution and made modifications to it.[[118]](#cite_note-122) The 1982 amendments apply to Quebec despite Quebec never having consented to it.[[119]](#cite_note-123)

Between 1982 and 1992, the Quebec government's attitude changed to prioritize reforming the federation. Attempts at constitutional amendments by the [Mulroney](/wiki/Brian_Mulroney "Brian Mulroney") and Bourassa governments ended in failure with the [Meech Lake Accord](/wiki/Meech_Lake_Accord "Meech Lake Accord") of 1987 and the [Charlottetown Accord](/wiki/Charlottetown_Accord "Charlottetown Accord") of 1992, resulting in the creation of the [Bloc Québécois](/wiki/Bloc_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Bloc Québécois").[[120]](#cite_note-124)[[121]](#cite_note-125) The failures also led to the re-election of the Parti Québécois in 1994, and the return to power of [Jacques Parizeau](/wiki/Jacques_Parizeau "Jacques Parizeau"), who had promised to hold a sovereignty referendum within a year of election. In 1995, Parizeau called a [referendum on Quebec's independence](/wiki/1995_Quebec_referendum "1995 Quebec referendum") from Canada. This consultation ended in a close outcome: 50.6% "no" and 49.4% "yes" (notably, over 60% of francophones voted "yes" and over 90% of anglophones voted "no").[[122]](#cite_note-1995_facts2-126)[[123]](#cite_note-127)[[124]](#cite_note-128)

In 1996, the federal government launched the Sponsorship Program to increase federal visibility in Quebec. In 2000, following the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada")'s decision on the [Reference Re Secession of Quebec](/wiki/Reference_Re_Secession_of_Quebec "Reference Re Secession of Quebec"), the Parliament of Canada passed a legal framework, called the [Clarity Act](/wiki/Clarity_Act "Clarity Act"), within which governments would act in another referendum. In 2002, the [Gomery commission](/wiki/Gomery_commission "Gomery commission") and media revealed the Sponsorship Program, in which $539,000 was illegally spent and where well-connected agencies received millions for minimal work. This [scandal](/wiki/Sponsorship_scandal "Sponsorship scandal") contributed to the Liberals' defeat in the [2006 federal election](/wiki/2006_Canadian_federal_election "2006 Canadian federal election").[[125]](#cite_note-129) On October 30, 2003, the National Assembly voted unanimously to affirm "that the people of Québec form a nation".[[126]](#cite_note-130) On November 27, 2006, the House of Commons followed with a [symbolic motion](/wiki/Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois_nation_motion "Québécois nation motion") declaring "that this House recognize that the Québécois form a nation within a united Canada."[[127]](#cite_note-131)

In 2007, the Parti Québécois was pushed back to official opposition in the National Assembly, with the Liberal party leading. During the [2011 Canadian federal elections](/wiki/2011_Canadian_federal_election "2011 Canadian federal election"), Quebec voters rejected the Bloc Québécois in favour of the previously minor [New Democratic Party (NDP)](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party "New Democratic Party"). As the NDP's logo is orange, this was called the "orange wave".[[128]](#cite_note-132) In 2012, the Liberal party, led by [Jean Charest](/wiki/Jean_Charest "Jean Charest"), announced an increase in student tuition fees. This spawned months-long protests involving over 300,000 students known as the [Maple Spring](/wiki/2012_Quebec_student_protests "2012 Quebec student protests"), ultimately leading to a rollback of the increases.[[129]](#cite_note-133) Also partially as a result, the Liberal party fell out of favour, letting the Parti Québécois regain power in 2012 and its leader, [Pauline Marois](/wiki/Pauline_Marois "Pauline Marois"), to become the first female premier of Quebec.[[130]](#cite_note-134) The Liberal Party of Quebec then returned to power in 2014.[[131]](#cite_note-135) Then, in 2018, the [Coalition Avenir Québec (CAQ)](/wiki/Coalition_Avenir_Qu%C3%A9bec "Coalition Avenir Québec") won the [provincial general elections](/wiki/2018_Quebec_general_election "2018 Quebec general election").[[132]](#cite_note-136)

Between 2020 and 2021, Quebec took measures against the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Quebec "COVID-19 pandemic in Quebec").[[133]](#cite_note-137) In 2022, the CAQ, led by Quebec's premier [François Legault](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Legault "François Legault"), increased its parliamentary majority in the [provincial general elections](/wiki/2022_Quebec_general_election "2022 Quebec general election").[[134]](#cite_note-138) In 2025, following the [implementation of tariffs](/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Tariffs in the second Trump administration") and [aggressive rhetoric](/wiki/2025_United_States_trade_war_with_Canada_and_Mexico "2025 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico") by the United States president [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), Quebecers decreased their travel to the US,[[135]](#cite_note-139) banned the sale of American alcohol,[[136]](#cite_note-140) and slightly reduced personal purchases of US items.[[137]](#cite_note-141)
