## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Quebec](/wiki/Geography_of_Quebec "Geography of Quebec")

See also: [List of rivers of Quebec](/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Quebec "List of rivers of Quebec") and [List of lakes of Quebec](/wiki/List_of_lakes_of_Quebec "List of lakes of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Map_of_Quebec.png/250px-Map_of_Quebec.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Quebec.png)

Map of Quebec

Located in the [eastern part](/wiki/Eastern_Canada "Eastern Canada") of Canada, Quebec occupies a territory nearly three times the size of [France](/wiki/France "France").[[138]](#cite_note-142) It holds an area of 1.5 million square kilometres (0.58 million square miles) and its borders are more than 12,000 km (7,500 mi) long.[[139]](#cite_note-Geography_of_Quebec_territory-143)[[140]](#cite_note-144) Most of Quebec is very sparsely populated.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The most populous [physiographic](/wiki/Physical_geography "Physical geography") region is the [Great Lakes–St. Lawrence Lowlands](/wiki/Great_Lakes%E2%80%93St._Lawrence_Lowlands "Great Lakes–St. Lawrence Lowlands"). The combination of rich soils and the lowlands' relatively warm climate makes this valley the most prolific agricultural area of Quebec. The rural part of the landscape is divided into narrow rectangular tracts of land that extend from the river and date back to the seigneurial system.

Quebec's [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography") is very different from one region to another due to the varying composition of the ground, the climate, and the proximity to water. More than 95% of Quebec's territory, including the [Labrador Peninsula](/wiki/Labrador_Peninsula "Labrador Peninsula"), lies within the [Canadian Shield](/wiki/Canadian_Shield "Canadian Shield").[[141]](#cite_note-naturalhistory2-145) It is generally a quite flat and exposed mountainous terrain interspersed with higher points such as the [Laurentian Mountains](/wiki/Laurentian_Mountains "Laurentian Mountains") in southern Quebec, the [Otish Mountains](/wiki/Otish_Mountains "Otish Mountains") in central Quebec and the [Torngat Mountains](/wiki/Torngat_Mountains "Torngat Mountains") near [Ungava Bay](/wiki/Ungava_Bay "Ungava Bay"). While low and medium altitude peaks extend from western Quebec to the far north, high altitudes mountains emerge in the [Capitale-Nationale](/wiki/Capitale-Nationale "Capitale-Nationale") region to the extreme east. Quebec's highest point at 1,652 metres (5,420 ft) is Mont d'Iberville, known in English as [Mount Caubvick](/wiki/Mount_Caubvick "Mount Caubvick").[[142]](#cite_note-146) In the Labrador Peninsula portion of the Shield, the far northern region of [Nunavik](/wiki/Nunavik "Nunavik") includes the Ungava Peninsula and consists of flat Arctic [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra") inhabited mostly by the Inuit. Further south is the [Eastern Canadian Shield taiga](/wiki/Eastern_Canadian_Shield_taiga "Eastern Canadian Shield taiga") ecoregion and the [Central Canadian Shield forests](/wiki/Central_Canadian_Shield_forests "Central Canadian Shield forests"). The [Appalachian](/wiki/Chaudi%C3%A8re-Appalaches "Chaudière-Appalaches") region has a narrow strip of ancient mountains along the southeastern border of Quebec.[[143]](#cite_note-Ecological_classification_of_Quebec_territory-147)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Chutes_%C3%A0_Michel.jpg/250px-Chutes_%C3%A0_Michel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chutes_%C3%A0_Michel.jpg)

Michel's falls on [Ashuapmushuan River](/wiki/Ashuapmushuan_River "Ashuapmushuan River") in [Saint-Félicien](/wiki/Saint-F%C3%A9licien,_Qu%C3%A9bec "Saint-Félicien, Québec"), [Saguenay–Lac-Saint-Jean](/wiki/Saguenay%E2%80%93Lac-Saint-Jean "Saguenay–Lac-Saint-Jean")

Quebec has one of the world's largest reserves of [fresh water](/wiki/Fresh_water "Fresh water"),[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMinistry_of_Environment_of_Quebec20025-148) occupying 12% of its surface[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBabin198639-149) and representing 3% of the world's [renewable fresh water](/wiki/Renewable_freshwater_resources "Renewable freshwater resources").[[146]](#cite_note-150) More than half a million lakes and 4,500 rivers[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMinistry_of_Environment_of_Quebec20025-148) empty into the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), through the [Gulf of Saint Lawrence](/wiki/Gulf_of_Saint_Lawrence "Gulf of Saint Lawrence") and the [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean"), by [James](/wiki/James_Bay "James Bay"), [Hudson](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay"), and Ungava bays. The largest inland body of water is the [Caniapiscau Reservoir](/wiki/Caniapiscau_Reservoir "Caniapiscau Reservoir"); [Lake Mistassini](/wiki/Lake_Mistassini "Lake Mistassini") is the largest natural lake.[[147]](#cite_note-CTQ-resC2-151) The [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") has some of the world's largest sustaining inland Atlantic ports. Since 1959, the [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway") has provided a navigable link between the Atlantic Ocean and the Great Lakes.

The [public lands](/wiki/Public_lands "Public lands") of Quebec cover approximately 92% of its territory, including almost all of the bodies of water. [Protected areas](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Quebec "Protected areas of Quebec") can be classified into about twenty different legal designations (ex. exceptional forest ecosystem, protected marine environment, [national park](/wiki/National_Parks_of_Quebec "National Parks of Quebec"), [biodiversity reserve](/wiki/Biodiversity_reserves_of_Quebec "Biodiversity reserves of Quebec"), wildlife reserve, [zone d'exploitation contrôlée](/wiki/Zone_d%27exploitation_contr%C3%B4l%C3%A9e "Zone d'exploitation contrôlée") (ZEC), etc.).[[148]](#cite_note-definition2-152) More than 2,500 sites in Quebec today are protected areas.[[149]](#cite_note-153) As of 2013, protected areas comprise 9.14% of Quebec's territory.[[150]](#cite_note-register2-154)

The ecological classification of Quebec territory established by the [Ministry of Forests, Wildlife and Parks](/wiki/Ministry_of_Energy_and_Natural_Resources_(Quebec) "Ministry of Energy and Natural Resources (Quebec)") 2021, is presented in nine levels, it includes the diversity of terrestrial [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") throughout Quebec while taking into account both the characteristics of the [vegetation](/wiki/Vegetation "Vegetation") (physiognomy, structure and composition) and the physical environment (relief, [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), [geomorphology](/wiki/Geomorphology "Geomorphology"), [hydrography](/wiki/Hydrography "Hydrography")).[[143]](#cite_note-Ecological_classification_of_Quebec_territory-147)

### Climate

Main article: [Ecological regions of Quebec](/wiki/Ecological_regions_of_Quebec "Ecological regions of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Quebec_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Quebec_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Quebec_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Quebec

In general, the climate of Quebec is cold and humid, with variations determined by latitude, maritime and elevation influences.[[151]](#cite_note-climat2-155) Because of the influence of both storm systems from the core of North America and the Atlantic Ocean, precipitation is abundant throughout the year, with most areas receiving more than 1,000 mm (39 in) of precipitation, including over 300 cm (120 in) of snow in many areas.[[152]](#cite_note-156) During the summer, severe weather patterns (such as [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") and severe [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm")) occur occasionally.[[153]](#cite_note-157)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Baie-Saint-Paul.jpg/250px-Baie-Saint-Paul.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baie-Saint-Paul.jpg)

[Baie-Saint-Paul](/wiki/Baie-Saint-Paul "Baie-Saint-Paul") during winter

Quebec is divided into four climatic zones: arctic, subarctic, humid continental and East maritime. From south to north, average temperatures range in summer between 25 and 5 °C (77 and 41 °F) and, in winter, between −10 and −25 °C (14 and −13 °F).[[154]](#cite_note-158)[[155]](#cite_note-159) In periods of intense heat and cold, temperatures can reach 35 °C (95 °F) in the summer[[156]](#cite_note-climatetabular2-160) and −40 °C (−40 °F) during the Quebec winter,[[156]](#cite_note-climatetabular2-160) Most of central Quebec, ranging from 51 to 58 degrees North has a [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") (Köppen *Dfc*).[[151]](#cite_note-climat2-155) Winters are long, very cold, and snowy, and among the coldest in eastern Canada, while summers are warm but very short due to the higher latitude and the greater influence of Arctic air masses. Precipitation is also somewhat less than farther south, except at some of the higher elevations. The northern regions of Quebec have an [arctic climate](/wiki/Climate_of_the_Arctic "Climate of the Arctic") (Köppen *ET*), with very cold winters and short, much cooler summers.[[151]](#cite_note-climat2-155) The primary influences in this region are the Arctic Ocean currents (such as the [Labrador Current](/wiki/Labrador_Current "Labrador Current")) and continental air masses from the High [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic").

The all-time record high temperature was 40.0 °C (104.0 °F) and the all-time record low was −51.0 °C (−59.8 °F).[[157]](#cite_note-161) The all-time record of the greatest precipitation in winter was established in winter 2007–2008, with more than five metres of snow in the area of Quebec City.[[158]](#cite_note-162) March 1971, however, saw the "[Century's Snowstorm](/wiki/Eastern_Canadian_Blizzard_of_March_1971 "Eastern Canadian Blizzard of March 1971")" with more than 40 cm (16 in) in Montreal to 80 cm (31 in) in [Mont Apica](/wiki/RCAF_Station_Mont_Apica "RCAF Station Mont Apica") of snow within 24 hours in many regions of southern Quebec. The winter of 2010 was the warmest and driest recorded in more than 60 years.[[159]](#cite_note-163)

### Flora and fauna

See also: [List of mammals of Quebec](/wiki/List_of_mammals_of_Quebec "List of mammals of Quebec"), [List of birds of Quebec](/wiki/List_of_birds_of_Quebec "List of birds of Quebec"), [List of reptiles of Quebec](/wiki/List_of_reptiles_of_Quebec "List of reptiles of Quebec"), [List of amphibians of Quebec](/wiki/List_of_amphibians_of_Quebec "List of amphibians of Quebec"), and [List of trees of Quebec](/wiki/List_of_trees_of_Quebec "List of trees of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Map_of_Quebec.png/250px-Map_of_Quebec.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Quebec.png)

Map of Quebec

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Ecoregions_quebec_map.svg/250px-Ecoregions_quebec_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ecoregions_quebec_map.svg)

Different forest areas of Quebec:

1. Middle Arctic Tundra

2. Low Arctic Tundra

3. Torngat Mountain Tundra

4. Eastern Canadian Shield Taiga

5. Southern Hudson Bay Taiga

6. Central Canadian Shield Forests

7. Eastern Canadian Forests

8. Eastern Forest/Boreal Transition

9. Eastern Great Lakes Lowland Forests

10. New England/Acadian Forests

11. Gulf of St. Lawrence Lowland Forests

Given the geology of the province and its different climates, there are a number of large areas of vegetation in Quebec. These areas, listed in order from the northernmost to the southernmost are: the [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra"), the [taiga](/wiki/Taiga "Taiga"), the [Canadian boreal forest](/wiki/Boreal_forest_of_Canada "Boreal forest of Canada") (coniferous), [mixed forest](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_and_mixed_forest "Temperate broadleaf and mixed forest") and [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") forest.[[141]](#cite_note-naturalhistory2-145) On the edge of Ungava Bay and Hudson Strait is the tundra, whose flora is limited to [lichen](/wiki/Lichen "Lichen") with less than 50 growing days per year. Further south, the climate is conducive to the growth of the [Canadian boreal forest](/wiki/Boreal_forest_of_Canada "Boreal forest of Canada"), bounded on the north by the taiga. Not as arid as the tundra, the taiga is associated with the subarctic regions of the Canadian Shield[[160]](#cite_note-types_veg2-164) and is characterized by a greater number of both plant (600) and animal (206) species. The taiga covers about 20% of the total area of Quebec.[[141]](#cite_note-naturalhistory2-145) The Canadian boreal forest is the northernmost and most abundant of the three forest areas in Quebec that straddle the Canadian Shield and the upper lowlands of the province. Given a warmer climate, the diversity of organisms is also higher: there are about 850 plant species and 280 vertebrate species. The [mixed forest](/wiki/Mixed_forest "Mixed forest") is a transition zone between the Canadian boreal forest and [deciduous forest](/wiki/Deciduous_forest "Deciduous forest"). This area contains a diversity of plant (1000) and [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") (350) species, despite relatively cool temperatures. The ecozone mixed forest is characteristic of the [Laurentians](/wiki/Laurentides "Laurentides"), the [Appalachians](/wiki/Chaudi%C3%A8re-Appalaches "Chaudière-Appalaches") and the eastern lowland forests.[[160]](#cite_note-types_veg2-164) The third most northern forest area is characterized by [deciduous forests](/wiki/Deciduous_forest "Deciduous forest"). Because of its climate, this area has the greatest diversity of species, including more than 1600 [vascular plants](/wiki/Vascular_plants "Vascular plants") and 440 vertebrates.

The total forest area of Quebec is estimated at 750,300 km2 (289,700 sq mi).[[161]](#cite_note-domaine_forestier2-165) From the [Abitibi-Témiscamingue](/wiki/Abitibi-T%C3%A9miscamingue "Abitibi-Témiscamingue") to the [North Shore](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-Nord "Côte-Nord"), the forest is composed primarily of conifers such as the *[Abies balsamea](/wiki/Abies_balsamea "Abies balsamea")*, the [jack pine](/wiki/Jack_pine "Jack pine"), the [white spruce](/wiki/Picea_glauca "Picea glauca"), the [black spruce](/wiki/Picea_mariana "Picea mariana") and the [tamarack](/wiki/Larix_laricina "Larix laricina"). The deciduous forest of the Great Lakes–St. Lawrence Lowlands is mostly composed of deciduous species such as the [sugar maple](/wiki/Acer_saccharum "Acer saccharum"), the [red maple](/wiki/Acer_rubrum "Acer rubrum"), the [white ash](/wiki/Fraxinus_americana "Fraxinus americana"), the [American beech](/wiki/Fagus_grandifolia "Fagus grandifolia"), the [butternut (white walnut)](/wiki/Juglans_cinerea "Juglans cinerea"), the [American elm](/wiki/Ulmus_americana "Ulmus americana"), the [basswood](/wiki/Tilia_americana "Tilia americana"), the [bitternut hickory](/wiki/Carya_cordiformis "Carya cordiformis") and the [northern red oak](/wiki/Quercus_rubra "Quercus rubra") as well as some conifers such as the [eastern white pine](/wiki/Eastern_white_pine "Eastern white pine") and the [northern whitecedar](/wiki/Thuja_occidentalis "Thuja occidentalis"). The distribution areas of the [paper birch](/wiki/Betula_papyrifera "Betula papyrifera"), the [trembling aspen](/wiki/Populus_tremuloides "Populus tremuloides") and the [mountain ash](/wiki/Sorbus "Sorbus") cover more than half of Quebec's territory.[[162]](#cite_note-166)

Biodiversity of the estuary and gulf of Saint Lawrence River[[163]](#cite_note-167) includes aquatic mammal wildlife, such as the [blue whale](/wiki/Blue_whale "Blue whale"), the [beluga](/wiki/Beluga_(whale) "Beluga (whale)"), the [minke whale](/wiki/Minke_whale "Minke whale") and the [harp seal](/wiki/Harp_seal "Harp seal") (earless seal). The Nordic marine animals include the [walrus](/wiki/Walrus "Walrus") and the [narwhal](/wiki/Narwhal "Narwhal").[[164]](#cite_note-168) Inland waters are populated by small to large freshwater fish, such as the [largemouth bass](/wiki/Largemouth_bass "Largemouth bass"), the [American pickerel](/wiki/American_pickerel "American pickerel"), the [walleye](/wiki/Walleye "Walleye"), the *[Acipenser oxyrinchus](/wiki/Acipenser_oxyrinchus "Acipenser oxyrinchus")*, the [muskellunge](/wiki/Muskellunge "Muskellunge"), the [Atlantic cod](/wiki/Atlantic_cod "Atlantic cod"), the [Arctic char](/wiki/Arctic_char "Arctic char"), the [brook trout](/wiki/Brook_trout "Brook trout"), the *[Microgadus tomcod](/wiki/Microgadus_tomcod "Microgadus tomcod")* (tomcod), the [Atlantic salmon](/wiki/Atlantic_salmon "Atlantic salmon"), and the [rainbow trout](/wiki/Rainbow_trout "Rainbow trout").[[165]](#cite_note-169)

Among the birds commonly seen in the southern part of Quebec are the [American robin](/wiki/American_robin "American robin"), the [house sparrow](/wiki/House_sparrow "House sparrow"), the [red-winged blackbird](/wiki/Red-winged_blackbird "Red-winged blackbird"), the [mallard](/wiki/Mallard "Mallard"), the [common grackle](/wiki/Common_grackle "Common grackle"), the [blue jay](/wiki/Blue_jay "Blue jay"), the [American crow](/wiki/American_crow "American crow"), the [black-capped chickadee](/wiki/Black-capped_chickadee "Black-capped chickadee"), some [warblers](/wiki/New_World_warbler "New World warbler") and [swallows](/wiki/Swallow "Swallow"), the [starling](/wiki/European_starling "European starling") and the [rock pigeon](/wiki/Rock_pigeon "Rock pigeon").[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrûlotte2009-170) Avian fauna includes birds of prey like the [golden eagle](/wiki/Golden_eagle "Golden eagle"), the [peregrine falcon](/wiki/Peregrine_falcon "Peregrine falcon"), the [snowy owl](/wiki/Snowy_owl "Snowy owl") and the [bald eagle](/wiki/Bald_eagle "Bald eagle"). Sea and semi-aquatic birds seen in Quebec are mostly the [Canada goose](/wiki/Canada_goose "Canada goose"), the [double-crested cormorant](/wiki/Double-crested_cormorant "Double-crested cormorant"), the [northern gannet](/wiki/Northern_gannet "Northern gannet"), the [European herring gull](/wiki/European_herring_gull "European herring gull"), the [great blue heron](/wiki/Great_blue_heron "Great blue heron"), the [sandhill crane](/wiki/Sandhill_crane "Sandhill crane"), the [Atlantic puffin](/wiki/Atlantic_puffin "Atlantic puffin") and the [common loon](/wiki/Common_loon "Common loon").[[167]](#cite_note-171)

The large land wildlife includes the [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer"), the [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose"), the [muskox](/wiki/Muskox "Muskox"), the [caribou (reindeer)](/wiki/Reindeer "Reindeer"), the [American black bear](/wiki/American_black_bear "American black bear") and the [polar bear](/wiki/Polar_bear "Polar bear"). The medium-sized land wildlife includes the [cougar](/wiki/Cougar "Cougar"), the [coyote](/wiki/Coyote "Coyote"), the [eastern wolf](/wiki/Eastern_wolf "Eastern wolf"), the [bobcat](/wiki/Bobcat "Bobcat"), the [Arctic fox](/wiki/Arctic_fox "Arctic fox"), the fox, etc. The small animals seen most commonly include the [eastern grey squirrel](/wiki/Eastern_grey_squirrel "Eastern grey squirrel"), the [snowshoe hare](/wiki/Snowshoe_hare "Snowshoe hare"), the [groundhog](/wiki/Groundhog "Groundhog"), the [skunk](/wiki/Skunk "Skunk"), the [raccoon](/wiki/Raccoon "Raccoon"), the [chipmunk](/wiki/Chipmunk "Chipmunk") and the [Canadian beaver](/wiki/North_American_beaver "North American beaver").

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of Quebec](/wiki/Government_of_Quebec "Government of Quebec") and [Politics of Quebec](/wiki/Politics_of_Quebec "Politics of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Quebec_City_%2814765614666%29.jpg/250px-Quebec_City_%2814765614666%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quebec_City_(14765614666).jpg)

The [Parliament Building](/wiki/Parliament_Building_(Quebec) "Parliament Building (Quebec)") in Quebec City

Quebec is founded on the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), and is both a [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") and a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") with [parliamentary regime](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system"). The head of government in Quebec is the [premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Quebec "Premier of Quebec") (called *premier ministre* in French), who leads the largest party in the [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") National Assembly (*Assemblée Nationale*) from which the [Executive Council of Quebec](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Quebec "Executive Council of Quebec") is appointed. The [Conseil du trésor](/wiki/Secr%C3%A9tariat_du_Conseil_du_tr%C3%A9sor_(Quebec) "Secrétariat du Conseil du trésor (Quebec)") supports the ministers of the Executive Council in their function of stewardship of the state. The [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Quebec "Lieutenant Governor of Quebec") represents the [King of Canada](/wiki/King_of_Canada "King of Canada").[[168]](#cite_note-172)[[169]](#cite_note-173)

Quebec has 78 [members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Canada) "Member of Parliament (Canada)") (MPs) in the [House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada").[[170]](#cite_note-174) They are elected in federal elections. At the level of the [Senate of Canada](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"), Quebec is represented by 24 senators, which are appointed on the advice of the [prime minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Canada "Prime minister of Canada").[[171]](#cite_note-175)

The Quebec government holds [administrative](/wiki/Administration_(government) "Administration (government)") and [police](/wiki/Police "Police") authority in its areas of [exclusive jurisdiction](/wiki/Exclusive_jurisdiction "Exclusive jurisdiction"). The Parliament of the [43rd legislature](/wiki/43rd_Quebec_Legislature "43rd Quebec Legislature") is made up of the following parties: [Coalition Avenir Québec](/wiki/Coalition_Avenir_Qu%C3%A9bec "Coalition Avenir Québec") (CAQ), [Parti libéral du Québec](/wiki/Parti_lib%C3%A9ral_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Parti libéral du Québec") (PLQ), [Québec solidaire](/wiki/Qu%C3%A9bec_solidaire "Québec solidaire") (QS) and [Parti Québécois](/wiki/Parti_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Parti Québécois") (PQ), as well as an [independent member](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Quebec#Current_standings "National Assembly of Quebec"). There are [25 official political parties in Quebec](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Quebec "List of political parties in Quebec").[[172]](#cite_note-176)

Quebec has a network of three offices for representing itself and defending its interests within Canada: one in Moncton for all provinces east, one in Toronto for all provinces west, and one in Ottawa for the federal government. These offices' mandate is to ensure an institutional presence of the Government of Quebec near other Canadian governments.[[173]](#cite_note-177)[[174]](#cite_note-178)

### Subdivisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Quebec](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Quebec "Administrative divisions of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Regions_administratives_du_Quebec.png/250px-Regions_administratives_du_Quebec.png)](/wiki/File:Regions_administratives_du_Quebec.png)

The [seventeen administrative regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Quebec "Regions of Quebec") of Quebec

Quebec's territory is divided into 17 [administrative regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Quebec "Regions of Quebec") as follows:[[175]](#cite_note-179)[[176]](#cite_note-180)

1. [Bas-Saint-Laurent](/wiki/Bas-Saint-Laurent "Bas-Saint-Laurent")
2. [Saguenay–Lac-Saint-Jean](/wiki/Saguenay%E2%80%93Lac-Saint-Jean "Saguenay–Lac-Saint-Jean")
3. [Capitale-Nationale](/wiki/Capitale-Nationale "Capitale-Nationale")
4. [Mauricie](/wiki/Mauricie "Mauricie")
5. [Estrie](/wiki/Estrie "Estrie")
6. [Montréal](/wiki/Urban_agglomeration_of_Montreal "Urban agglomeration of Montreal")
7. [Outaouais](/wiki/Outaouais "Outaouais")
8. [Abitibi-Témiscamingue](/wiki/Abitibi-T%C3%A9miscamingue "Abitibi-Témiscamingue")
9. [Côte-Nord](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-Nord "Côte-Nord")
10. [Nord-du-Québec](/wiki/Nord-du-Qu%C3%A9bec "Nord-du-Québec")
11. [Gaspésie–Îles-de-la-Madeleine](/wiki/Gasp%C3%A9sie%E2%80%93%C3%8Eles-de-la-Madeleine "Gaspésie–Îles-de-la-Madeleine")
12. [Chaudière-Appalaches](/wiki/Chaudi%C3%A8re-Appalaches "Chaudière-Appalaches")
13. [Laval](/wiki/Laval,_Quebec "Laval, Quebec")
14. [Lanaudière](/wiki/Lanaudi%C3%A8re "Lanaudière")
15. [Laurentides](/wiki/Laurentides "Laurentides")
16. [Montérégie](/wiki/Mont%C3%A9r%C3%A9gie "Montérégie")
17. [Centre-du-Québec](/wiki/Centre-du-Qu%C3%A9bec "Centre-du-Québec")

The province also has the following divisions:

* 4 territories ([Abitibi](/wiki/Abitibi_County,_Quebec "Abitibi County, Quebec"), [Ashuanipi](/wiki/Ashuanipi "Ashuanipi"), [Mistassini](/wiki/Rivi%C3%A8re-Mistassini,_Quebec "Rivière-Mistassini, Quebec") and [Nunavik](/wiki/Nunavik "Nunavik")) which group together the lands that once formed the [District of Ungava](/wiki/District_of_Ungava "District of Ungava")
* 36 [judicial districts](/wiki/Judicial_districts_of_Quebec "Judicial districts of Quebec")
* 73 *circonscriptions foncières*
* 125 [electoral districts](/w/index.php?title=Electoral_districts_of_Quebec&action=edit&redlink=1 "Electoral districts of Quebec (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circonscription_%C3%A9lectorale_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "fr:Circonscription électorale du Québec")][[177]](#cite_note-181)

For municipal purposes, Quebec is composed of:

* 1,117 [local municipalities](/wiki/Local_government_in_Quebec "Local government in Quebec") of various [types](/wiki/Types_of_municipalities_in_Quebec "Types of municipalities in Quebec"):
  + 11 [agglomerations](/wiki/Urban_agglomerations_of_Quebec "Urban agglomerations of Quebec") (*agglomérations*) grouping 42 of these local municipalities
  + 45 [boroughs](/wiki/List_of_boroughs_in_Quebec "List of boroughs in Quebec") (*arrondissements*) within 8 of these local municipalities
* 89 [regional county municipalities](/wiki/Regional_county_municipality "Regional county municipality") or RCMs (*municipalités régionales de comté, MRC*)
* 2 [metropolitan communities](/wiki/Metropolitan_Community_(Quebec) "Metropolitan Community (Quebec)") (*communautés métropolitaines*)
* the regional [Kativik](/wiki/Kativik_Regional_Government "Kativik Regional Government") administration
* the [unorganised territories](/wiki/List_of_unorganized_territories_in_Quebec "List of unorganized territories in Quebec")[[178]](#cite_note-182)

### Ministries and policies

Quebec's constitution is enshrined in a series of social and cultural traditions that are defined in a set of judicial judgments and legislative documents, including the *Loi sur l'Assemblée Nationale* ("Law on the National Assembly"), the *Loi sur l'éxecutif* ("Law on the Executive"), and the *Loi électorale du Québec* ("Electoral Law of Quebec").[[179]](#cite_note-183) Other notable examples include the [Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Quebec_Charter_of_Human_Rights_and_Freedoms "Quebec Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms"), the [Charter of the French language](/wiki/Charter_of_the_French_language "Charter of the French language"), and the [Civil Code of Quebec](/wiki/Civil_Code_of_Quebec "Civil Code of Quebec").[[180]](#cite_note-CCQ2-184)

Quebec's international policy is founded upon the [Gérin-Lajoie doctrine](/w/index.php?title=G%C3%A9rin-Lajoie_doctrine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gérin-Lajoie doctrine (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doctrine_G%C3%A9rin-Lajoie "fr:Doctrine Gérin-Lajoie")],[[181]](#cite_note-185) formulated in 1965. While Quebec's [Ministry of International Relations](/wiki/Ministry_of_International_Relations_(Quebec) "Ministry of International Relations (Quebec)") coordinates international policy, [Quebec's general delegations](/wiki/Quebec_Government_Offices "Quebec Government Offices") are the main interlocutors in foreign countries. Quebec is the only Canadian province that has set up a ministry to exclusively embody the state's powers for international relations.[[182]](#cite_note-186)

Since 2006, Quebec has adopted a green plan to meet the objectives of the [Kyoto Protocol](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol") regarding climate change.[[183]](#cite_note-187) The [Ministry of Sustainable Development, Environment, and Fight Against Climate Change](/wiki/Ministry_of_Sustainable_Development,_Environment,_and_Fight_Against_Climate_Change "Ministry of Sustainable Development, Environment, and Fight Against Climate Change") (MELCC) is the primary entity responsible for the application of environmental policy. The [Société des établissements de plein air du Québec](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_des_%C3%A9tablissements_de_plein_air_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Société des établissements de plein air du Québec") (SEPAQ) is the main body responsible for the management of national parks and wildlife reserves.[[184]](#cite_note-188) Nearly 500,000 people took part in a climate protest on the streets of Montreal in 2019.[[185]](#cite_note-189)

Agriculture in Quebec has been subject to [agricultural zoning](/wiki/Agricultural_zoning "Agricultural zoning") regulations since 1978.[[186]](#cite_note-190) Faced with the problem of expanding [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"), agricultural zones were created to ensure the protection of fertile land, which make up 2% of Quebec's total area. [Quebec's forests](/w/index.php?title=Quebec%27s_forests&action=edit&redlink=1 "Quebec's forests (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/For%C3%AAt_au_Qu%C3%A9bec "fr:Forêt au Québec")] are essentially public property. The calculation of annual cutting possibilities is the responsibility of the *Bureau du forestier en chef*.[[187]](#cite_note-191) The [Union des producteurs agricoles](/wiki/Union_des_producteurs_agricoles "Union des producteurs agricoles") (UPA) seeks to protect the interests of its members, including forestry workers, and works jointly with the [Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food](/wiki/Ministry_of_Agriculture,_Fisheries_and_Food_(Quebec) "Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food (Quebec)") (MAPAQ) and the [Ministry of Energy and Natural Resources](/wiki/Ministry_of_Energy_and_Natural_Resources_(Quebec) "Ministry of Energy and Natural Resources (Quebec)").

The *Ministère de l'Emploi et de la Solidarité sociale du Québec* has the mandate to oversee social and workforce developments through Emploi-Québec and its local employment centres (CLE).[[188]](#cite_note-192) This ministry is also responsible for managing the *Régime québécois d'assurance parentale* (QPIP) as well as last-resort financial support for people in need. The [Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail](/w/index.php?title=Commission_des_normes,_de_l%27%C3%A9quit%C3%A9,_de_la_sant%C3%A9_et_de_la_s%C3%A9curit%C3%A9_du_travail&action=edit&redlink=1 "Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commission_des_normes,_de_l%27%C3%A9quit%C3%A9,_de_la_sant%C3%A9_et_de_la_s%C3%A9curit%C3%A9_du_travail "fr:Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail")] (CNESST) is the main body responsible for labour laws in Quebec[[189]](#cite_note-193) and for enforcing agreements concluded between unions of employees and their employers.[[190]](#cite_note-194)

[Revenu Québec](/wiki/Revenu_Qu%C3%A9bec "Revenu Québec") is the body responsible for collecting taxes. It takes its revenue through a [progressive income tax](/wiki/Progressive_tax "Progressive tax"), a 9.975% sales tax,[[191]](#cite_note-195) various other provincial taxes (ex. carbon, corporate and capital gains taxes), [equalization payments](/wiki/Equalization_payments_in_Canada "Equalization payments in Canada"), transfer payments from other provinces, and direct payments.[[192]](#cite_note-196) By some measures Quebec residents are the most taxed;[[193]](#cite_note-197) a 2012 study indicated that "Quebec companies pay 26 per cent more in taxes than the Canadian average".[[194]](#cite_note-198)

Quebec's immigration philosophy is based on the principles of pluralism and [interculturalism](/wiki/Interculturalism "Interculturalism").The *Ministère de l'Immigration et des Communautés culturelles du Québec* is responsible for the selection and integration of immigrants.[[195]](#cite_note-199) Programs favour immigrants who know French, have a low risk of becoming criminals and have in-demand skills.

Quebec's health and social services network is administered by the [Ministry of Health and Social Services](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_and_Social_Services_(Quebec) "Ministry of Health and Social Services (Quebec)"). It is composed of 95 *réseaux locaux de services* (RLS; 'local service networks') and 18 *agences de la santé et des services sociaux* (ASSS; 'health and social services agencies'). Quebec's health system is supported by the *[Régie de l'assurance maladie du Québec](/wiki/R%C3%A9gie_de_l%27assurance_maladie_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Régie de l'assurance maladie du Québec")* (RAMQ) which works to maintain the accessibility of services for all citizens of Quebec.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

The *Ministère de la Famille et des Aînés du Québec* operate [centres de la petite enfance](/w/index.php?title=Centres_de_la_petite_enfance&action=edit&redlink=1 "Centres de la petite enfance (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Centre_de_la_petite_enfance "fr:Centre de la petite enfance")] (CPEs; 'centres for young children'). Quebec [provides universal low-fee childcare](/wiki/Child_care_in_Canada#Child_care_in_Québec "Child care in Canada") for all children under 12. [Quebec's education system](/wiki/Education_in_Quebec "Education in Quebec") is administered by the [Ministry of Education and Higher Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_and_Higher_Education_(Quebec) "Ministry of Education and Higher Education (Quebec)") ([primary](/wiki/Primary_school "Primary school") and [secondary](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school") schools), the *Ministère de l'Enseignement supérieur* ([CEGEP](/wiki/CEGEP "CEGEP")) and the *Conseil supérieure de l'Education du Québec* (universities and colleges).[[197]](#cite_note-201) In 2012, the annual cost for postsecondary [tuition](/wiki/Tuition_payments "Tuition payments") was CA$2,168 (€1,700) – less than half of Canada's average tuition. Part of the reason for this is that tuition fees were frozen to a relatively low level when CEGEPS were created during the Quiet Revolution. When Jean Charest's government decided in 2012 to sharply increase university fees, [students protests](/wiki/2012_Quebec_student_protests "2012 Quebec student protests") erupted.[[198]](#cite_note-202) Because of these protests, Quebec's tuition fees remain relatively low.

### External relationships

Quebec's closest international partner is the United States, with which it shares a long and positive history. Products of [American culture](/wiki/American_culture "American culture") like songs, movies, fashion and food strongly affect Québécois culture.

Quebec has a historied relationship with France, as Quebec was a part of the French Empire and both regions share a language. The [Fédération France-Québec](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_France-Qu%C3%A9bec&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fédération France-Québec (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_France-Qu%C3%A9bec "fr:Fédération France-Québec")] and the [Francophonie](/wiki/Francophonie "Francophonie") are a few of the tools used for relations between Quebec and France. In [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), a *place du Québec* was inaugurated in 1980.[[199]](#cite_note-203) Quebec also has a historied relationship with the United Kingdom, having been a part of the British Empire. Quebec and the UK share the same head of state, [King Charles III](/wiki/King_Charles_III "King Charles III").

Quebec has a [network of 32 offices](/wiki/Quebec_Government_Offices "Quebec Government Offices") in 18 countries. These offices serve the purpose of representing Quebec in foreign countries and are overseen by Quebec's [Ministry of International Relations](/wiki/Ministry_of_International_Relations_(Quebec) "Ministry of International Relations (Quebec)"). Quebec, like other Canadian provinces, also maintains representatives in some Canadian embassies and consulates general. As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quebec&action=edit), the Government of Quebec had delegates-general ([agents-general](/wiki/Agents-general "Agents-general")) in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [London](/wiki/London "London"), [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), Paris and [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"); delegates to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), and [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"); and offices headed by directors offering more limited services in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Dakar](/wiki/Dakar "Dakar"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), and [Washington](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."). In addition, there are the equivalent of [honorary consuls](/wiki/Consul_(representative)#honorary_consul "Consul (representative)"), titled *antennes*, in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [Qingdao](/wiki/Qingdao "Qingdao"), [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), and [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley").

Quebec also has a representative to [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") and participates in the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States").[[200]](#cite_note-204) Quebec is a member of the [Assemblée parlementaire de la Francophonie](/wiki/Assembl%C3%A9e_parlementaire_de_la_Francophonie "Assemblée parlementaire de la Francophonie") and of the [Organisation internationale de la francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_francophonie "Organisation internationale de la francophonie").

## Law

Main articles: [Quebec law](/wiki/Quebec_law "Quebec law") and [Bar of Quebec](/wiki/Bar_of_Quebec "Bar of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Edifice_Ernest-Cormier_14.JPG/250px-Edifice_Ernest-Cormier_14.JPG)](/wiki/File:Edifice_Ernest-Cormier_14.JPG)

The [Édifice Ernest-Cormier](/wiki/%C3%89difice_Ernest-Cormier "Édifice Ernest-Cormier") is the courthouse for the [Quebec Court of Appeal](/wiki/Quebec_Court_of_Appeal "Quebec Court of Appeal") in Montreal.

Quebec law is the shared responsibility of the [federal](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") and [provincial government](/wiki/Government_of_Quebec "Government of Quebec"). The federal government is responsible for [criminal law](/wiki/Criminal_law "Criminal law"), foreign affairs and laws relating to the regulation of Canadian commerce, interprovincial transportation, and telecommunications.[[201]](#cite_note-CF-205) The provincial government is responsible for [private law](/wiki/Private_law "Private law"), the administration of justice, and several social domains, such as social assistance, healthcare, education, and natural resources.[[201]](#cite_note-CF-205)

Quebec law is influenced by two judicial traditions ([civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") and [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law")) and four classic [sources of law](/wiki/Sources_of_law "Sources of law") ([legislation](/wiki/Legislation "Legislation"), [case law](/wiki/Case_law "Case law"), doctrine and [customary law](/wiki/Customary_law "Customary law")).[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKélada197021-206) Private law in Quebec affects all relationships between individuals ([natural](/wiki/Natural_person "Natural person") or [juridical](/wiki/Juridical_person "Juridical person") persons) and is largely under the jurisdiction of the [Parliament of Quebec](/wiki/Parliament_of_Quebec "Parliament of Quebec"). The [Parliament of Canada](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada") also influences Quebec private law, in particular through its power over banks, bankruptcy, marriage, divorce and [maritime law](/wiki/Maritime_law "Maritime law").[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrunTremblayBrouillet2008474–491-207) The *[Droit civil du Québec](/w/index.php?title=Droit_civil_du_Qu%C3%A9bec&action=edit&redlink=1 "Droit civil du Québec (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Droit_civil_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "fr:Droit civil du Québec")]* is the primary component of Quebec's private law and is [codified](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") in the [Civil Code of Quebec](/wiki/Civil_Code_of_Quebec "Civil Code of Quebec").[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÉmondLauzière200338-208) Public law in Quebec is largely derived from the common law tradition.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrunTremblayBrouillet20089,_28-209) Quebec [constitutional law](/wiki/Constitutional_law "Constitutional law") governs the rules surrounding the Quebec government, the Parliament of Quebec and Quebec's courts. Quebec [administrative law](/wiki/Administrative_law "Administrative law") governs relations between individuals and the Quebec public administration. Quebec also has some limited jurisdiction over criminal law. Finally, Quebec, like the federal government, has [tax law](/wiki/Tax_law "Tax law") power.[[206]](#cite_note-210) Certain portions of Quebec law are considered mixed. This is the case, for example, with [human rights and freedoms](/wiki/Human_rights_and_freedoms "Human rights and freedoms") which are governed by the [Quebec Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Quebec_Charter_of_Human_Rights_and_Freedoms "Quebec Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms"), a Charter which applies to both government and citizens.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÉmondLauzière200339–40-211)[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrunTremblayBrouillet2008943–945-212)

English is not an official language in [Quebec law](/wiki/Quebec_law "Quebec law").[[209]](#cite_note-charteroffrench2-213) However, both English and French are required by the [Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867") for the enactment of laws and regulations, and any person may use English or French in the National Assembly and the courts. The books and records of the National Assembly must also be kept in both languages.[[210]](#cite_note-214)[[211]](#cite_note-215)

### Courts

Although Quebec is a civil law jurisdiction, it does not follow the pattern of other civil law systems which have court systems divided by subject matter. Instead, the court system follows the English model of unitary courts of general jurisdiction. The provincial courts have jurisdiction to decide matters under [provincial law](/wiki/Law_of_Canada "Law of Canada") as well as [federal law](/wiki/Federal_law "Federal law"), including [civil](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)"), [criminal](/wiki/Criminal_law "Criminal law") and [constitutional matters](/wiki/Constitutional_law "Constitutional law").[[212]](#cite_note-216) The major exception to the principle of general jurisdiction is that the [Federal Court](/wiki/Federal_Court_(Canada) "Federal Court (Canada)") and [Federal Court of Appeal](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Appeal "Federal Court of Appeal") have exclusive jurisdiction over some areas of federal law, such as review of federal administrative bodies, federal taxes, and matters relating to national security.[[213]](#cite_note-217)

The Quebec courts are organized in a pyramid. At the bottom, there are the [municipal courts](/wiki/Municipal_court "Municipal court"), the Professions Tribunal, the Human Rights Tribunal, and administrative tribunals. Decisions of those bodies can be reviewed by the two trial courts, the [Court of Quebec](/wiki/Court_of_Quebec "Court of Quebec") the [Superior Court of Quebec](/wiki/Superior_Court_of_Quebec "Superior Court of Quebec"). The Court of Quebec is the main criminal trial court, and also a court for small civil claims. The Superior Court is a trial court of general jurisdiction, in both criminal and civil matters. The decisions of those courts can be appealed to the [Quebec Court of Appeal](/wiki/Quebec_Court_of_Appeal "Quebec Court of Appeal"). Finally, if the case is of great importance, it may be appealed to the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada").

The Court of Appeal serves two purposes. First, it is the general court of appeal for all legal issues from the lower courts. It hears appeals from the trial decisions of the Superior Court and the Quebec Court. It also can hear appeals from decisions rendered by those two courts on appeals or judicial review matters relating to the municipal courts and administrative tribunals.[[214]](#cite_note-218) Second, but much more rarely, the Court of Appeal possesses the power to respond to [reference questions](/wiki/Reference_question "Reference question") posed to it by the [Quebec Cabinet](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Quebec "Executive Council of Quebec"). The Court of Appeal renders more than 1,500 judgments per year.[[215]](#cite_note-219)

### Law enforcement

The [Sûreté du Québec](/wiki/S%C3%BBret%C3%A9_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Sûreté du Québec") is the main police force of Quebec. The Sûreté du Québec can also serve a support and coordination role with other police forces, such as with municipal police forces or with the [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police") (RCMP).[[216]](#cite_note-220)[[217]](#cite_note-221) The RCMP has the power to enforce certain federal laws in Quebec. However, given the existence of the Sûreté du Québec, its role is more limited than in the other provinces.[[218]](#cite_note-222)

Municipal police, such as the [Service de police de la Ville de Montréal](/wiki/Service_de_police_de_la_Ville_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Service de police de la Ville de Montréal") and the [Service de police de la Ville de Québec](/wiki/Service_de_police_de_la_Ville_de_Qu%C3%A9bec "Service de police de la Ville de Québec"), are responsible for law enforcement in their municipalities. The Sûreté du Québec fulfils the role of municipal police in the 1038 municipalities that do not have a municipal police force.[[219]](#cite_note-223) The Indigenous communities of Quebec have their own police forces.[[220]](#cite_note-224)

For offences against provincial or federal laws in Quebec (including the [Criminal Code](/wiki/Criminal_Code_(Canada) "Criminal Code (Canada)")), the [Director of Criminal and Penal Prosecutions](/wiki/Director_of_Criminal_and_Penal_Prosecutions "Director of Criminal and Penal Prosecutions") is responsible for prosecuting offenders in court through [Crown attorneys](/wiki/Crown_attorney "Crown attorney"). The [Department of Justice of Canada](/wiki/Department_of_Justice_(Canada) "Department of Justice (Canada)") also has the power to prosecute offenders, but only for offences against specific federal laws (ex. selling [narcotics](/wiki/Narcotic "Narcotic")). Quebec is responsible for operating the [prison system](/wiki/Ministry_of_Public_Security_(Quebec) "Ministry of Public Security (Quebec)") for sentences of less than two years, and the federal government operates [penitentiaries](/wiki/Incarceration_in_Canada "Incarceration in Canada") for sentences of two years or more.[[221]](#cite_note-225)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Quebec](/wiki/Demographics_of_Quebec "Demographics of Quebec") and [Demographic history of Quebec](/wiki/Demographic_history_of_Quebec "Demographic history of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Quebec_Population_Density_2021.svg/500px-Quebec_Population_Density_2021.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Quebec_Population_Density_2021.svg)

Population density map of Quebec

In the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), Quebec's population was determined to be 8,501,833, a 4.1% increase from its 2016 population of 8,164,361. With a land area of 1,356,625.27 km2 (523,795.95 sq mi), it had a population density of 6.0/km2 (15.6/sq mi) in 2016. Quebec accounted for a little under 23% of the Canadian population. The [largest cities in Quebec](/wiki/List_of_population_centres_in_Quebec "List of population centres in Quebec") are [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") (1,762,976), [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") (538,738), [Laval](/wiki/Laval,_Quebec "Laval, Quebec") (431,208), and [Gatineau](/wiki/Gatineau,_Quebec "Gatineau, Quebec") (281,501).[[222]](#cite_note-226)

In 2016, Quebec's median age was 41.2 years. As of 2020, 20.8% of the population was younger than 20, 59.5% was aged between 20 and 64, and 19.7% was 65 or older. In 2019, Quebec witnessed an increase in the number of births compared to the year before (84,200 vs 83,840) and had a [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") of about 1.6 children born per woman. As of 2020, the average life expectancy was 82.3 years. Quebec in 2019 registered its highest rate of population growth since 1972, with an increase of 110,000 people, mostly because of the arrival of a high number of immigrants. As of 2019, most international immigrants were from China, India and France.[[223]](#cite_note-227) In 2016, 30% of the population possessed a [postsecondary](/wiki/Postsecondary "Postsecondary") degree or diploma. Most residents, particularly couples, are [property owners](/wiki/Property_owner "Property owner"). In 2016, 80% of both property owners and renters considered their housing to be "unaffordable".[[224]](#cite_note-habitation-228) In the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), 29.3% of Quebec's population stated their ancestry was of Canadian origin and 21.1% stated their ancestry was of French origin.[[225]](#cite_note-229) As of 2021, 18% of Quebec's population belonged to a [visible minority](/wiki/Visible_minorities "Visible minorities") group.[[226]](#cite_note-230)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Quebec](/wiki/Religion_in_Quebec "Religion in Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Basilique_Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupr%C3%A9_12.jpg/250px-Basilique_Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupr%C3%A9_12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Basilique_Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupr%C3%A9_12.jpg)

The [Basilica of Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré](/wiki/Basilica_of_Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupr%C3%A9 "Basilica of Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré")

According to the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), the most commonly cited religions in Quebec were:[[227]](#cite_note-2021censusB-231)

* [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Canada "Christianity in Canada") (5,385,240 residents, or 64.8%)
* [Irreligion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Canada "Irreligion in Canada") (2,267,720 or 27.3%)
* [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Canada "Islam in Canada") (421,710 or 5.1%)
* [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_Canada "Judaism in Canada") (84,530 or 1.0%)
* [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Canada "Buddhism in Canada") (48,365 or 0.6%)
* [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Canada "Hinduism in Canada") (47,390 or 0.6%)
* [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Canada "Sikhism in Canada") (23,345 or 0.3%)
* [Indigenous spirituality](/wiki/Mythologies_of_the_indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Mythologies of the indigenous peoples of the Americas") (3,790 or <0.1%)
* Other (26,385 or 0.3%)

The [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") has long occupied a central and integral place in Quebec society since the foundation of Quebec City in 1608. However, since the [Quiet Revolution](/wiki/Quiet_Revolution "Quiet Revolution"), which secularized Quebec, irreligion has been growing significantly.[[228]](#cite_note-232) In 2025, there was a reported rise in adult baptisms and an increased number of baptisms of people over the age of 7.[[229]](#cite_note-233) The Catholic Church of Quebec described the recent increase in baptisms among adults and adolescents as "surprising phenomenon has been gaining momentum" and that interest in baptism in the Catholic faith is "especially present" in young people and in adults from immigrant backgrounds.[[230]](#cite_note-234)

Religions other than Christianity, Judaism and indigenous faiths were not present in Quebec before the 20th century. They started establishing a small presence following the passing of the *Immigration Act of 1962*. Islam in particular has grown rapidly since the 1990s due to high immigration levels. Its number of adherents increased from 44,930 (0.6% of the population) in 1991 to 421,715 (5.1%) in 2021.[[231]](#cite_note-235)[[232]](#cite_note-236)

The oldest [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") in North America is the [Cathedral-Basilica of Notre-Dame de Québec](/wiki/Cathedral-Basilica_of_Notre-Dame_de_Qu%C3%A9bec "Cathedral-Basilica of Notre-Dame de Québec"). Its construction began in 1647, when it was known under the name *Notre-Dame-de-la-Paix*, and it was finished in 1664.[[233]](#cite_note-237) The most frequented place of worship in Quebec is the [Basilica of Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré](/wiki/Basilica_of_Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupr%C3%A9 "Basilica of Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré"). This basilica welcomes millions of visitors each year. [Saint Joseph's Oratory](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Oratory "Saint Joseph's Oratory") is the largest place of worship in the world dedicated to [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph"). Many pilgrimages include places such as [Saint Benedict Abbey](/wiki/Saint_Benedict_Abbey,_Quebec "Saint Benedict Abbey, Quebec"), [Sanctuaire Notre-Dame-du-Cap](/w/index.php?title=Sanctuaire_Notre-Dame-du-Cap&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sanctuaire Notre-Dame-du-Cap (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanctuaire_Notre-Dame-du-Cap "fr:Sanctuaire Notre-Dame-du-Cap")], [Notre-Dame de Montréal Basilica](/wiki/Notre-Dame_de_Montr%C3%A9al_Basilica "Notre-Dame de Montréal Basilica"), [Marie-Reine-du-Monde de Montréal Basilica-Cathedral](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_the_World_Cathedral "Mary, Queen of the World Cathedral"), [Saint-Michel Basilica-Cathedral](/wiki/Saint-Michel_Basilica-Cathedral "Saint-Michel Basilica-Cathedral"), and [Saint-Patrick's Basilica](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Basilica,_Montreal "St. Patrick's Basilica, Montreal"). Another important place of worship in Quebec is the Anglican Holy Trinity Cathedral, which was erected between 1800 and 1804. It was the first Anglican cathedral built outside the British Isles.[[234]](#cite_note-238)

### Language

Main article: [Language demographics of Quebec](/wiki/Language_demographics_of_Quebec "Language demographics of Quebec")

See also: [French language in Canada](/wiki/French_language_in_Canada "French language in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Quebec_langues.png/250px-Quebec_langues.png)](/wiki/File:Quebec_langues.png)

|  |
| --- |
| Linguistic map of the province of Quebec (source: Statistics Canada, 2006 census)  Francophone majority, less than 33% Anglophone  Francophone majority, more than 33% Anglophone  Anglophone majority, more than 33% Francophone  Anglophone majority, less than 33% Francophone  Data not available |

Quebec differs from other Canadian provinces in that [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") is its sole [official](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") language, while [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") predominates in the [rest of Canada](/wiki/Rest_of_Canada "Rest of Canada").[[235]](#cite_note-239) French is the [common language](/wiki/Common_language "Common language"), understood and spoken by 93.7% of the population according to the [2021 Census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"),[[236]](#cite_note-language2021-240) and is the sole native language of 74.8% of the population (or slightly more than 6.5 million residents) and a native language (alone or in combination with others) of 77.8%.[[237]](#cite_note-TP-241) This makes Quebec the only Canadian province whose population is mainly [Francophone](/wiki/Francophone "Francophone"). [Quebec French](/wiki/Quebec_French "Quebec French") is the umbrella term for local [variants](/wiki/Variation_(linguistics) "Variation (linguistics)") of the language. Canada is estimated to be home to roughly 30 regional French accents,[[238]](#cite_note-quebeccultureblog.com2-242)[[239]](#cite_note-243) 17 of which can be found in Quebec.[[240]](#cite_note-244) 42.2% of Quebec's population with a French mother tongue can converse in English, the predominant language of the rest of Canada.[[241]](#cite_note-245)

The [Office québécois de la langue française](/wiki/Office_qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois_de_la_langue_fran%C3%A7aise "Office québécois de la langue française") oversees the application of linguistic policies respecting French on the territory, jointly with the [Superior Council of the French Language](/wiki/Conseil_sup%C3%A9rieur_de_la_langue_fran%C3%A7aise_(Quebec) "Conseil supérieur de la langue française (Quebec)") and the [Commission de toponymie du Québec](/wiki/Commission_de_toponymie_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Commission de toponymie du Québec"). The foundation for these linguistic policies was created in 1968 by the [Gendron Commission](/wiki/Commission_of_Inquiry_on_the_Situation_of_the_French_Language_and_Linguistic_Rights_in_Quebec "Commission of Inquiry on the Situation of the French Language and Linguistic Rights in Quebec") and they have been accompanied the [Charter of the French language](/wiki/Charter_of_the_French_language "Charter of the French language") ("Bill 101") since 1977. The policies are in effect to protect Quebec from being assimilated by its English-speaking neighbours (the rest of Canada and the United States)[[242]](#cite_note-246)[[243]](#cite_note-247) and were also created to rectify historical injustice between the Francophone majority and Anglophone minority, the latter of which were favoured since Quebec was a colony of the British Empire.[[244]](#cite_note-248) Quebec remains, alongside [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), the only major Francophone dominant regions in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").[[245]](#cite_note-249)[[246]](#cite_note-250)[[247]](#cite_note-251)

[Anglo-Quebecers](/wiki/Anglo-Quebecers "Anglo-Quebecers"), a name for residents whose main language is English, constitute the second largest linguistic group in Quebec. In 2021, [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") was the sole mother tongue of 7.6% of Quebec residents, and was a native language (alone or in combination with others) of 10.0%.[[237]](#cite_note-TP-241) Anglo-Quebecers reside mainly in the west of the island of Montreal ([West Island](/wiki/West_Island "West Island")), [downtown Montreal](/wiki/Downtown_Montreal "Downtown Montreal") and the [Pontiac](/wiki/Pontiac_Regional_County_Municipality "Pontiac Regional County Municipality").

Three families of Indigenous languages encompassing eleven languages exist in Quebec: the [Algonquian language family](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages") ([Abenaki](/wiki/Abenaki_language "Abenaki language"), [Algonquin](/wiki/Algonquin_language "Algonquin language"), [Maliseet-passamaquoddy](/wiki/Malecite-Passamaquoddy_language "Malecite-Passamaquoddy language"), [Mi'kmaq](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq_language "Mi'kmaq language"), and the [linguistic continuum](/wiki/Linguistic_continuum "Linguistic continuum") of [Atikamekw](/wiki/Atikamekw_language "Atikamekw language"), [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language"), [Innu-aimun](/wiki/Innu_language "Innu language"), and [Naskapi](/wiki/Naskapi_language "Naskapi language")), the [Inuit–Aleut language family](/wiki/Eskimo%E2%80%93Aleut_languages "Eskimo–Aleut languages") (Nunavimmiutitut, an [Inuktitut dialect](/wiki/Inuit_grammar "Inuit grammar") spoken by the [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") of [Nord-du-Québec](/wiki/Nord-du-Qu%C3%A9bec "Nord-du-Québec")), and the [Iroquoian language family](/wiki/Iroquoian_languages "Iroquoian languages") ([Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_language "Mohawk language") and [Wendat](/wiki/Wendat_language "Wendat language")). In the 2016 census, 50,895 people said they knew at least one Indigenous language[[248]](#cite_note-252) and 45,570 people declared having an Indigenous language as their mother tongue.[[249]](#cite_note-253) In Quebec, most Indigenous languages are transmitted quite well from one generation to the next with a mother tongue retention rate of 92%.[[250]](#cite_note-254)

As of the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Canadian_census "2016 Canadian census"), the most common immigrant languages claimed as a native language were [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") (2.5% of the total population), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") (1.9%), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") (1.4%), Creole languages (mainly [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole")) (0.8%), and [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (0.6%).[[251]](#cite_note-255)

As of the [2021 Canadian Census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), the ten most spoken languages in the province were [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") (spoken by 7,786,735 people, or 93.72% of the population), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") (4,317,180 or 51.96%), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") (453,905 or 5.46%), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") (343,675 or 4.14%), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") (168,040 or 2.02%), [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole") (118,010 or 1.42%), [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (80,520 or 0.97%), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") (65,605 or 0.8%), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") (55,485 or 0.7%), and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") (50,375 or 0.6%).[[236]](#cite_note-language2021-240) The question on knowledge of languages allows for multiple responses.

### Indigenous peoples

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Quebec](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Quebec "Indigenous peoples in Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Communaut%C3%A9s_autochtones_du_Qu%C3%A9bec.png/250px-Communaut%C3%A9s_autochtones_du_Qu%C3%A9bec.png)](/wiki/File:Communaut%C3%A9s_autochtones_du_Qu%C3%A9bec.png)

Map of aboriginal communities in Quebec, this includes [reserves](/wiki/Indian_reserve "Indian reserve"), [settlements](/wiki/Indian_settlement "Indian settlement") and [northern villages](/wiki/Types_of_municipalities_in_Quebec#Aboriginal_local_municipal_units "Types of municipalities in Quebec"):

Algonquin
