In 2021, the Indigenous population of Quebec numbered 205,010 (2.5% of the population), including 15,800 [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), 116,550 [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") people, and 61,010 [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis").[[252]](#cite_note-256) There is an undercount, as some Indian bands regularly refuse to participate in Canadian censuses. In 2016, the [Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_people "Mohawk people") reserves of [Kahnawake](/wiki/Kahnawake "Kahnawake") and [Doncaster 17](/wiki/Doncaster,_Quebec "Doncaster, Quebec") along with the [Indian settlement](/wiki/Indian_settlement "Indian settlement") of [Kanesatake](/wiki/Kanesatake "Kanesatake") and [Lac-Rapide](/wiki/Rapid_Lake "Rapid Lake"), a reserve of the [Algonquins of Barriere Lake](/wiki/Algonquins_of_Barriere_Lake "Algonquins of Barriere Lake"), were not counted.[[253]](#cite_note-257)

The Inuit of Quebec live mainly in [Nunavik](/wiki/Nunavik "Nunavik") in [Nord-du-Québec](/wiki/Nord-du-Qu%C3%A9bec "Nord-du-Québec"). They make up the majority of the population living north of the 55th parallel. There are ten First Nations ethnic groups in Quebec: the [Abenaki](/wiki/Abenaki "Abenaki"), the [Algonquin](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people"), the [Attikamek](/wiki/Attikamek "Attikamek"), the [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree"), the [Wolastoqiyik](/wiki/Wolastoqiyik "Wolastoqiyik"), the [Mi'kmaq](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq "Mi'kmaq"), the [Innu](/wiki/Innu "Innu"), the [Naskapi](/wiki/Naskapi "Naskapi"), the [Wendat](/wiki/Wendat_Nation "Wendat Nation") and the [Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawks "Mohawks"). The Mohawks were once part of the [Iroquois Confederacy](/wiki/Iroquois_Confederacy "Iroquois Confederacy").
Aboriginal rights were enunciated in the [Indian Act](/wiki/Indian_Act "Indian Act") and adopted at the end of the 19th century. This act confines [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") within the [reserves](/wiki/Indian_reserve "Indian reserve") created for them. The Indian Act is still in effect today.[[254]](#cite_note-258) In 1975, the [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree"), [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") and the Quebec government agreed to an agreement called the [James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement](/wiki/James_Bay_and_Northern_Quebec_Agreement "James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement") that would extended indigenous rights beyond reserves, and to over two-thirds of Quebec's territory. Because this extension was enacted without the participation of the [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada"), the extended indigenous rights only exist in Quebec. In 1978, the [Naskapis](/wiki/Naskapi "Naskapi") joined the agreement when the [Northeastern Quebec Agreement](/wiki/Northeastern_Quebec_Agreement "Northeastern Quebec Agreement") was signed. Discussions have been underway with the Innu of the [Côte-Nord](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-Nord "Côte-Nord") and [Saguenay–Lac-Saint-Jean](/wiki/Saguenay%E2%80%93Lac-Saint-Jean "Saguenay–Lac-Saint-Jean") for the potential creation of a similar autonomy in two new distinct territories that would be called *Innu Assi* and *Nitassinan*.[[255]](#cite_note-259)

A few political institutions have also been created over time:

* The [Assembly of First Nations Quebec-Labrador](/wiki/Assembly_of_First_Nations_Quebec-Labrador "Assembly of First Nations Quebec-Labrador")[[256]](#cite_note-260)
* The [Grand Council of the Crees](/wiki/Grand_Council_of_the_Crees "Grand Council of the Crees")[[257]](#cite_note-261)
* The [Makivik Corporation](/wiki/Makivik_Corporation "Makivik Corporation")[[258]](#cite_note-262)

### Acadians

The subject of [Acadians in Quebec](/wiki/Acadians_in_Quebec "Acadians in Quebec") is an important one as more than a million people in Quebec are of [Acadian](/wiki/Acadians "Acadians") descent, with roughly 4.8 million people possessing one or multiple Acadian ancestors in their genealogy tree, because a large number of Acadians had fled [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") to take refuge in Quebec during the [Great Upheaval](/wiki/Great_Upheaval "Great Upheaval"). Furthermore, more than a million people have a [patronym](/wiki/Patronym "Patronym") of Acadian origin.[[259]](#cite_note-263)[[260]](#cite_note-264)[[261]](#cite_note-265)[[262]](#cite_note-266)

Quebec houses Acadian communities. Acadians mainly live on the [Magdalen Islands](/wiki/Magdalen_Islands "Magdalen Islands") and in [Gaspesia](/wiki/Gaspesia "Gaspesia"), but about thirty other communities are present elsewhere in Quebec, mostly in the [Côte-Nord](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-Nord "Côte-Nord") and [Centre-du-Québec](/wiki/Centre-du-Qu%C3%A9bec "Centre-du-Québec") regions. An Acadian community in Quebec can be called a "Cadie", "Petite Cadie" or "Cadien".[[263]](#cite_note-267)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Quebec](/wiki/Economy_of_Quebec "Economy of Quebec")

Quebec has an [advanced](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"), [market-based](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy"), and [open economy](/wiki/Open_economy "Open economy"). In 2022, its [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) was [US$50,000](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_provinces_and_territories_by_gross_domestic_product "List of Canadian provinces and territories by gross domestic product") per person at [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity").[[264]](#cite_note-268) The economy of Quebec is the [46th largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") behind [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") and [29th](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") for GDP per person.[[265]](#cite_note-269)[[266]](#cite_note-270) Quebec represents 19% of the [GDP of Canada](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_provinces_and_territories_by_gross_domestic_product "List of Canadian provinces and territories by gross domestic product"). The provincial [debt-to-GDP ratio](/wiki/Debt-to-GDP_ratio "Debt-to-GDP ratio") peaked at 51% in 2012–2013, and declined to 43% in 2021.[[267]](#cite_note-271)

Like most [industrialized countries](/wiki/Industrialized_countries "Industrialized countries"), the economy is based mainly on the [services sector](/wiki/Services_sector "Services sector"). Quebec's economy has traditionally been fuelled by abundant natural resources and a well-developed infrastructure, but has undergone significant change over the past decade.[[268]](#cite_note-272) Firmly grounded in the [knowledge economy](/wiki/Knowledge_economy "Knowledge economy"), Quebec has one of the highest growth rates of GDP in Canada. The knowledge sector represents about 31% of Quebec's GDP.[[269]](#cite_note-273) In 2011, Quebec experienced faster growth of its [research-and-development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") (R&D) spending than other Canadian provinces.[[270]](#cite_note-274) Quebec's spending in R&D in 2011 was equal to 2.63% of GDP, above the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") average of 1.8%.[[271]](#cite_note-275) The percentage spent on research and technology is the highest in Canada and higher than the averages for the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development") and the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") countries.[[272]](#cite_note-276)

Some of the most important companies from Quebec are: [Bombardier](/wiki/Bombardier_Inc "Bombardier Inc"), [Desjardins](/wiki/Desjardins_Group "Desjardins Group"), the [National Bank of Canada](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Canada "National Bank of Canada"), the [Jean Coutu Group](/wiki/Jean_Coutu_Group "Jean Coutu Group"), [Transcontinental média](/wiki/Transcontinental_(company) "Transcontinental (company)"), [Quebecor](/wiki/Quebecor "Quebecor"), the [Métro Inc.](/wiki/Metro_Inc. "Metro Inc.") food retailers, [Hydro-Québec](/wiki/Hydro-Qu%C3%A9bec "Hydro-Québec"), the [Société des alcools du Québec](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_des_alcools_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Société des alcools du Québec"), the [Bank of Montreal](/wiki/Bank_of_Montreal "Bank of Montreal"), [Saputo](/wiki/Saputo_Inc "Saputo Inc"), the [Cirque du Soleil](/wiki/Cirque_du_Soleil "Cirque du Soleil"), the [Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec](/wiki/Caisse_de_d%C3%A9p%C3%B4t_et_placement_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec"), the Normandin restaurants, and [Vidéotron](/wiki/Vid%C3%A9otron "Vidéotron").

### Exports and imports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/EXPORT_quebec_USA_Chine.JPG/330px-EXPORT_quebec_USA_Chine.JPG)](/wiki/File:EXPORT_quebec_USA_Chine.JPG)

Quebec's exports to the international market. The United States is the country which buys the most exports from Quebec by far. (2011)

Thanks to the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO) and the [North American Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/North_American_Free_Trade_Agreement "North American Free Trade Agreement") (NAFTA), Quebec had, as of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Quebec&action=edit), experienced an increase in its exports and in its ability to compete on the international market. International exchanges contribute to the strength of the Quebec economy.[[273]](#cite_note-PQ-277) NAFTA is especially advantageous as it gives Quebec, among other things, access to a market of 130 million consumers within a radius of 1,000 kilometres.

In 2008, Quebec's exports to other provinces in Canada and abroad totalled 157.3 billion [CND$](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar"), or 51.8% of Quebec's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP). Of this total, 60.4% were international exports, and 39.6% were interprovincial exports. The breakdown by destination of international merchandise exports is: United States (72.2%), Europe (14.4%), Asia (5.1%), Middle East (2.7%), Central America (2.3%), South America (1.9%), Africa (0.8%) and Oceania (0.7%).[[273]](#cite_note-PQ-277)

In 2008, Quebec imported $178 billion worth of goods and services, or 58.6% of its GDP. Of this total, 62.9% of goods were imported from international markets, while 37.1% of goods were interprovincial imports. The breakdown by origin of international merchandise imports is as follows: United States (31.1%), Europe (28.7%), Asia (17.1%), Africa (11.7%), South America (4.5%), Central America (3.7%), Middle East (1.3%) and Oceania (0.7%).[[273]](#cite_note-PQ-277)

### Primary sector

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Centrale_de_Beauharnois_-_2014.jpg/250px-Centrale_de_Beauharnois_-_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Centrale_de_Beauharnois_-_2014.jpg)

The [Beauharnois generating station](/wiki/Beauharnois_generating_station "Beauharnois generating station"), operated by [Hydro-Québec](/wiki/Hydro-Qu%C3%A9bec "Hydro-Québec")

Quebec produces most of Canada's [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") and is the second biggest hydroelectricity producer in the world (2019).[[274]](#cite_note-278) Because of this, Quebec has been described as a potential [clean energy](/wiki/Clean_energy "Clean energy") superpower.[[275]](#cite_note-279) In 2019, Quebec's electricity production amounted to 214 [terawatt](/wiki/Terawatt "Terawatt")-hours (TWh), 95% of which comes from hydroelectric power stations, and 4.7% of which come from [wind energy](/wiki/Wind_energy "Wind energy"). The public company [Hydro-Québec](/wiki/Hydro-Qu%C3%A9bec "Hydro-Québec") occupies a dominant position in the production, transmission and distribution of electricity in Quebec. Hydro-Québec operates 63 hydroelectric power stations and 28 large reservoirs.[[276]](#cite_note-hydroquebec.com2-280) Because of the remoteness of Hydro-Québec's [TransÉnergie](/wiki/Hydro-Qu%C3%A9bec%27s_electricity_transmission_system "Hydro-Québec's electricity transmission system") division, it operates the largest electricity transmission network in North America. Quebec stands out for its use of [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy"). In 2008, electricity ranked as the main form of energy used in Quebec (41.6%), followed by [oil](/wiki/Oil "Oil") (38.2%) and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") (10.7%).[[277]](#cite_note-281) In 2017, 47% of all energy came from renewable sources.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitmorePineau20208-282) The Quebec government's energy policy seeks to build, by 2030, a [low carbon economy](/wiki/Low_carbon_economy "Low carbon economy").

In 2011, the [mining industry](/wiki/Mining_industry "Mining industry") accounted for 6.3% of Quebec's GDP[[279]](#cite_note-283) and it employed about 50,000 people in 158 companies.[[280]](#cite_note-284) It has around 30 mines, 158 exploration companies and 15 primary processing industries. While many metallic and industrial minerals are exploited, the main ones are gold, iron, copper and [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"). Others include: [titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium"), [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos"), silver, [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium") and nickel, among many others.[[281]](#cite_note-285) Quebec is also as a major source of diamonds.[[282]](#cite_note-286) Since 2002, Quebec has seen an increase in its mineral explorations. In 2003, the value of mineral exploitation reached $3.7 billion.[[283]](#cite_note-287)

The [agri-food industry](/wiki/Agrifood "Agrifood") plays an important role in the economy of Quebec, with meat and [dairy products](/wiki/Dairy_product "Dairy product") being the two main sectors. It accounts for 8% of the Quebec's GDP and generate $19.2 billion. In 2010, this industry generated 487,000 jobs in agriculture, fisheries, manufacturing of food, beverages and tobacco and food distribution.[[284]](#cite_note-288)

### Secondary sector

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Bombardier_CSeries_mockup.jpg/250px-Bombardier_CSeries_mockup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bombardier_CSeries_mockup.jpg)

A mockup of the [Airbus A220](/wiki/Airbus_A220 "Airbus A220") (formerly the Bombardier CSeries), originally developed by [Bombardier Aerospace](/wiki/Bombardier_Aerospace "Bombardier Aerospace")

In 2021, Quebec's [aerospace industry](/wiki/Aerospace_industry "Aerospace industry") employed 35,000 people and its sales totalled C$15.2 billion – the world's 6th largest.[[285]](#cite_note-289) Many aerospace companies are active here, including [CMC Electronics](/wiki/CMC_Electronics "CMC Electronics"), [Bombardier](/wiki/Bombardier_Aerospace "Bombardier Aerospace"), [Pratt & Whitney Canada](/wiki/Pratt_%26_Whitney_Canada "Pratt & Whitney Canada"), [Héroux-Devtek](/wiki/H%C3%A9roux-Devtek "Héroux-Devtek"), [Rolls-Royce](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Holdings "Rolls-Royce Holdings"), [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric"), [Bell Textron](/wiki/Bell_Textron "Bell Textron"), [L3Harris](/wiki/L3Harris "L3Harris"), [Safran](/wiki/Safran "Safran"), [SONACA](/wiki/SONACA "SONACA"), [CAE Inc.](/wiki/CAE_Inc. "CAE Inc."), and [Airbus](/wiki/Airbus "Airbus"), among others. Montreal is globally considered one of the aerospace industry's great centres, and several international aviation organisations seat here.[[286]](#cite_note-290) Both Aéro Montréal and the CRIAQ were created to assist aerospace companies.[[287]](#cite_note-291)[[288]](#cite_note-292)

The [pulp and paper industry](/wiki/Pulp_and_paper_industry "Pulp and paper industry") accounted for 3.1% of Quebec's GDP in 2007 [[289]](#cite_note-293) and generated annual shipments valued at more than $14 billion.[[290]](#cite_note-294) This industry employs 68,000 people in several regions of Quebec.[[291]](#cite_note-295) It is also the main -and in some circumstances only- source of manufacturing activity in more than 250 municipalities in the province. The forest industry has slowed in recent years because of the [softwood lumber dispute](/wiki/United_States_%E2%80%93_Canada_softwood_lumber_dispute "United States – Canada softwood lumber dispute").[[292]](#cite_note-296) In 2020, this industry represented 8% of Quebec's exports.[[293]](#cite_note-297)

As Quebec has few significant deposits of [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel"),[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitmorePineau202010-298) all [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") are imported. [Refiners'](/wiki/Refinery "Refinery") sourcing strategies have varied over time and have depended on market conditions. In the 1990s, Quebec purchased much of its oil from the [North Sea](/wiki/Brent_Crude "Brent Crude"). Since 2015, it now consumes almost exclusively the crude produced in [western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada") and the United States.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitmorePineau20209-299) Quebec's two active refineries have a total capacity of 402,000 barrels per day, greater than local needs which stood at 365,000 barrels per day in 2018.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitmorePineau202010-298)

Thanks to hydroelectricity, Quebec is the world's fourth largest [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminium_foil "Aluminium foil") producer and creates 90% of Canadian aluminum. Three companies make aluminum here: [Rio Tinto](/wiki/Rio_Tinto_(corporation) "Rio Tinto (corporation)"), [Alcoa](/wiki/Alcoa "Alcoa") and Aluminium Alouette. Their 9 *alumineries* produce 2,9 million tons of aluminum annually and employ 30,000 workers.[[296]](#cite_note-300)

### Tertiary sector

The [finance and insurance](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") sector employs more than 168,000 people. Of this number, 78,000 are employed by the banking sector, 53,000 by the insurance sector and 20,000 by the securities and investment sector.[[297]](#cite_note-301) The [Bank of Montreal](/wiki/Bank_of_Montreal "Bank of Montreal"), founded in 1817 in Montreal, was Quebec's first bank but, like many other large banks, its central branch is now in Toronto. Several banks remain based in Quebec [National Bank of Canada](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Canada "National Bank of Canada"), the [Desjardins Group](/wiki/Desjardins_Group "Desjardins Group") and the [Laurentian Bank](/wiki/Laurentian_Bank_of_Canada "Laurentian Bank of Canada").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Chateau_Frontenac_2.jpg/250px-Chateau_Frontenac_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chateau_Frontenac_2.jpg)

The [Château Frontenac](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_Frontenac "Château Frontenac") is the most photographed hotel in the world.

The [tourism industry](/wiki/Tourism_in_Quebec "Tourism in Quebec") is a major sector in Quebec. The [Ministry of Tourism](/wiki/Ministry_of_Tourism_(Quebec) "Ministry of Tourism (Quebec)") ensures the development of this industry under the commercial name "Bonjour Québec".[[298]](#cite_note-302) Quebec is the second most important province for tourism in Canada, receiving 21.5% of tourists' spending (2021).[[299]](#cite_note-303) The industry provides employment to over 400,000 people.[[300]](#cite_note-304) These employees work in the more than 29,000 tourism-related businesses in Quebec, most of which are restaurants or hotels. 70% of tourism-related businesses are located in or close to Montreal or Quebec City. It is estimated that, in 2010, Quebec welcomed 25.8 million tourists. Of these, 76.1% came from Quebec, 12.2% from the [rest of Canada](/wiki/Rest_of_Canada "Rest of Canada"), 7.7% from the United States and 4.1% from other countries. Annually, tourists spend more than $6.7 billion in Quebec's tourism industry.[[301]](#cite_note-305)

Approximately 1.1 million Quebecers work in the field of science and technology.[[302]](#cite_note-306) In 2007, the [Government of Quebec](/wiki/Government_of_Quebec "Government of Quebec") launched the Stratégie québécoise de la recherche et de l'innovation (SQRI) aiming to promote development through research, science and technology. The government hoped to create a strong culture of innovation in Quebec for the next decades and to create a [sustainable economy](/wiki/Sustainable_economy "Sustainable economy").[[303]](#cite_note-307)

Quebec's [IT](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") sector has 7,600 businesses and employs 140,000 people.[[304]](#cite_note-308)[[305]](#cite_note-309)[[306]](#cite_note-310) Its most developed sectors are [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications"), multimedia and video game software, computer services, microelectronics, and the components sector. There are currently 115 telecommunications companies established in the province, including [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola"), [Ericsson](/wiki/Ericsson "Ericsson") and Mitec.[[307]](#cite_note-311) The multimedia and video game sector has been growing fast since the early 2000s. The Digital Alliance, which claims 191 active members in video games, online education, mobility and Internet services, estimates the annual revenue of the sector at $827 million in 2014.[[308]](#cite_note-312) The microelectronics sector is made up of more than 100 companies employing 13,000 people. Computer services, software development, and consulting engineering employ 60,000 skilled workers. While the largest IT employers are [CMC Electronics](/wiki/CMC_Electronics "CMC Electronics"), [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), and [Matrox](/wiki/Matrox "Matrox"), many other tech companies are present here, including [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft_Montreal "Ubisoft Montreal"), [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts"), [Microids](/wiki/Microids "Microids"), [Strategy First](/wiki/Strategy_First "Strategy First"), [Eidos](/wiki/Eidos-Montr%C3%A9al "Eidos-Montréal"), [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision"), [A2M](/wiki/Artificial_Mind_and_Movement "Artificial Mind and Movement"), [Frima Studio](/wiki/Frima_Studio "Frima Studio"), etc.[[309]](#cite_note-313)

Montreal is ranked fourth in North America for the number of jobs in the pharmaceutical sector.[[310]](#cite_note-emergex2-314)[[311]](#cite_note-315)

## Education

Main article: [Education in Quebec](/wiki/Education_in_Quebec "Education in Quebec")

See also: [List of universities in Quebec](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Quebec "List of universities in Quebec"), [List of colleges in Quebec](/wiki/List_of_colleges_in_Quebec "List of colleges in Quebec"), and [List of schools in Quebec](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Quebec "List of schools in Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Edifice_quebec_INRS.jpg/250px-Edifice_quebec_INRS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edifice_quebec_INRS.jpg)

The [Institut national de la recherche scientifique](/wiki/Institut_national_de_la_recherche_scientifique "Institut national de la recherche scientifique") helps to advance scientific knowledge and to train a new generation of students in various scientific and technological sectors.

The education system of Quebec, administered by the government of Quebec's [Ministry of Education and Higher Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_and_Higher_Education_(Quebec) "Ministry of Education and Higher Education (Quebec)"), differs from those of other Canadian provinces. The province has five levels of education: first [preschool, then primary school, then secondary school](/w/index.php?title=Preschool,_primary_and_secondary_education_in_Quebec&action=edit&redlink=1 "Preschool, primary and secondary education in Quebec (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89ducation_pr%C3%A9scolaire,_primaire_et_secondaire_au_Qu%C3%A9bec "fr:Éducation préscolaire, primaire et secondaire au Québec")]; then [CEGEP](/wiki/CEGEP "CEGEP") (see [College education in Quebec](/wiki/College_education_in_Quebec "College education in Quebec")); and finally [university or college](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Quebec "Higher education in Quebec"). Attached to these levels are the options to also attend [professional development](/wiki/Professional_development "Professional development") opportunities, [classes for adults](/wiki/Andragogy "Andragogy"), and [continuing education](/wiki/Continuing_education "Continuing education"). For every level of teaching, there exists a public network and private network: the public network is financed by taxes while the private options must be paid for by the student. In 2020, [school boards](/wiki/School_boards "School boards") were replaced by [school service centres](/wiki/School_service_centre "School service centre").[[312]](#cite_note-316)

All universities in Quebec exist by virtue of laws adopted by the [National Assembly of Quebec](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Quebec "National Assembly of Quebec") in 1967 during the [Quiet Revolution](/wiki/Quiet_Revolution "Quiet Revolution"). Their financing mostly comes from public taxes, but the laws under which they operate grants them more autonomy than other levels of education.[[313]](#cite_note-317)

### Research

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Apollo_11_Lunar_Module_Eagle_in_landing_configuration_in_lunar_orbit_from_the_Command_and_Service_Module_Columbia.jpg/250px-Apollo_11_Lunar_Module_Eagle_in_landing_configuration_in_lunar_orbit_from_the_Command_and_Service_Module_Columbia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apollo_11_Lunar_Module_Eagle_in_landing_configuration_in_lunar_orbit_from_the_Command_and_Service_Module_Columbia.jpg)

In 1969, [Héroux-Devtek](/wiki/H%C3%A9roux-Devtek "Héroux-Devtek") designed and manufactured the [undercarriage](/wiki/Landing_gear "Landing gear") of the [Apollo Lunar Module](/wiki/Apollo_Lunar_Module "Apollo Lunar Module").

Quebec is considered one of world leaders in [fundamental scientific research](/wiki/Basic_research "Basic research"), having produced ten [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") in either physics, chemistry, or medicine.[[314]](#cite_note-318) It is also considered one of the world leaders in sectors such as aerospace, information technology, biotechnology and pharmaceuticals, and therefore plays a significant role in the world's scientific and technological communities.[[315]](#cite_note-319) Between 2000 and 2011, Quebec had over 9,469 scientific publications in biomedical research and engineering.[[316]](#cite_note-320) The contribution of Quebec in science and technology represented approximately 1% of the research worldwide between the 1980s and 2009.[[317]](#cite_note-321)

The province is one of the world leaders in the field of [space science](/wiki/Space_science "Space science") and contributed to important discoveries in this field.[[318]](#cite_note-322) The [Canadian Space Agency](/wiki/Canadian_Space_Agency "Canadian Space Agency") was established in Quebec due to its major role in this research field. A total of four Quebecers have been in space since the creation of the CSA: [Marc Garneau](/wiki/Marc_Garneau "Marc Garneau"), [Julie Payette](/wiki/Julie_Payette "Julie Payette"), and [David Saint-Jacques](/wiki/David_Saint-Jacques "David Saint-Jacques") as CSA astronauts, plus [Guy Laliberté](/wiki/Guy_Lalibert%C3%A9 "Guy Laliberté") as a private citizen who paid for his trip. Quebec has also contributed to the creation of some Canadian [artificial satellites](/wiki/Artificial_satellite "Artificial satellite") including [SCISAT-1](/wiki/SCISAT-1 "SCISAT-1"), [ISIS](/wiki/ISIS_(satellite) "ISIS (satellite)"), [Radarsat-1](/wiki/Radarsat-1 "Radarsat-1") and [Radarsat-2](/wiki/Radarsat-2 "Radarsat-2").[[319]](#cite_note-323)[[320]](#cite_note-324)[[321]](#cite_note-325)

Quebec ranks among the world leaders in the field of [life science](/wiki/Life_science "Life science").[[310]](#cite_note-emergex2-314) Quebec has more than 450 biotechnology and pharmaceutical companies which together employ more than 25,000 people and 10,000 highly qualified researchers.[[310]](#cite_note-emergex2-314)

## Infrastructure

### Transportation

Main articles: [List of airports in Quebec](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Quebec "List of airports in Quebec"), [List of Quebec railways](/wiki/List_of_Quebec_railways "List of Quebec railways"), and [List of Quebec provincial highways](/wiki/List_of_Quebec_provincial_highways "List of Quebec provincial highways")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Cmarcoux3.JPG/250px-Cmarcoux3.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cmarcoux3.JPG)

The ferry N.M. *Camille-Marcoux*, of the *Société des traversiers du Québec*

Development and security of land transportation in Canada are provided by [Transports Québec](/wiki/Transports_Qu%C3%A9bec "Transports Québec").[[322]](#cite_note-326) Other organizations, such as the [Canadian Coast Guard](/wiki/Canadian_Coast_Guard "Canadian Coast Guard") and [Nav Canada](/wiki/Nav_Canada "Nav Canada"), provide the same service for the sea and air transportation. The *Commission des transports du Québec* works with the freight carriers and the public transport.

The *réseau routier québécois* (Quebec road network) is managed by the [Société de l'assurance automobile du Québec](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_l%27assurance_automobile_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Société de l'assurance automobile du Québec") (SAAQ; Quebec Automobile Insurance Corporation) and consists of about 185,000 km (115,000 mi) of highways and national, regional, local, collector and forest roads. In addition, Quebec has almost 12,000 bridges, tunnels, retaining walls, culverts and other structures[[323]](#cite_note-327) such as the [Quebec Bridge](/wiki/Quebec_Bridge "Quebec Bridge"), the [Laviolette Bridge](/wiki/Laviolette_Bridge "Laviolette Bridge") and the [Louis-Hippolyte Lafontaine Bridge–Tunnel](/wiki/Louis-Hippolyte_Lafontaine_Bridge%E2%80%93Tunnel "Louis-Hippolyte Lafontaine Bridge–Tunnel").

In the waters of the Saint Lawrence there are eight deep-water ports for the transhipment of goods. In 2003, 3886 cargo and 9.7 million tonnes of goods transited the Quebec portion of the [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway").[[324]](#cite_note-transport-qc2-328)

Concerning rail transport, Quebec has 6,678 km (4,150 mi) of railways[[325]](#cite_note-329) integrated in the large North American network. Although primarily intended for the transport of goods through companies such as the [Canadian National](/wiki/Canadian_National "Canadian National") (CN) and the [Canadian Pacific](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific "Canadian Pacific") (CP), the Quebec railway network is also used by inter-city passengers via [Via Rail Canada](/wiki/Via_Rail_Canada "Via Rail Canada") and [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"). In April 2012, plans were unveiled for the construction of an 800 km (497 mi) railway running north from [Sept-Îles](/wiki/Sept-%C3%8Eles,_Quebec "Sept-Îles, Quebec"), to support mining and other resource extraction in the Labrador Trough.[[326]](#cite_note-330)

Quebec's air network includes 43 airports that offer scheduled services on a daily basis.[[324]](#cite_note-transport-qc2-328) In addition, the Government of Quebec owns airports and heliports to increase the accessibility of local services to communities in the [Basse-Côte-Nord](/wiki/Basse-C%C3%B4te-Nord "Basse-Côte-Nord") and northern regions.[[327]](#cite_note-331)

Various other transport networks crisscross the province of Quebec, including hiking trails, snowmobile trails and bike paths. The [Green Road](/wiki/Route_Verte "Route Verte") is the largest at nearly 4,000 km (2,500 mi) in length.[[328]](#cite_note-332)

### Healthcare

Quebec has a health policy that emphasizes prevention, is based on the analysis of health-related data, and evolves with the needs of the population. Similar to other developed economies, the public health policies implemented in Quebec have extended the life expectancy of its population since the mid-20th century.[[329]](#cite_note-333)

Health and social services are part of the same administration. The Quebec health system is also public, which means that the [government](/wiki/%C3%89tat_qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "État québécois") acts as the main insurer and administrator, that funding is provided by general taxation, and that patients have access to care regardless of their income level.

There are 34 health establishments in Quebec, 22 of which are an [Integrated Health and Social Services Centre](/w/index.php?title=Integrated_Health_and_Social_Services_Centre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Integrated Health and Social Services Centre (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Centre_int%C3%A9gr%C3%A9_de_sant%C3%A9_et_de_services_sociaux "fr:Centre intégré de santé et de services sociaux")] (CISSS). They ensure the distribution of different services on the territories they are assigned to. Quebec has approximately 140 hospitals for general or specialised care (CHSGS). Quebec also has other types of establishments in its healthcare system, such as *Centre local de services communautaires* (CLSC), *Centre d'hébergement et de soins de longue durée* (CHSLD), *Centre de réadaptation* and *Centre de protection de l'enfance et de la jeunesse*. Finally, there are private healthcare establishments (paid for directly by the patient) like [Groupe de médecine de famille](/w/index.php?title=Groupe_de_m%C3%A9decine_de_famille&action=edit&redlink=1 "Groupe de médecine de famille (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Groupes_de_m%C3%A9decine_de_famille "fr:Groupes de médecine de famille")], pharmacies, private clinics, dentists, community organisations and retirement homes.[[330]](#cite_note-334)

A 2021 Ipsos poll found that 85% of Quebecers agree that their health care system is too bureaucratic to respond to the needs of the population[[331]](#cite_note-335) and in 2023 found that less than half of Quebecers are satisfied with the provincial health care system.[[332]](#cite_note-336)

### Housing

Main article: [Housing crisis in Quebec](/wiki/Housing_crisis_in_Quebec "Housing crisis in Quebec")

In 2021, 59.9% of Quebec's residents were property owners.[[333]](#cite_note-337) In 2019, among property owners, 34% were couples with kids, 33% were couples without children, 22% lived alone, 8% were single parents, and 3% were something else. Among renters, 16% were couples with kids, 13% were couples without children, 51% lived alone, 13% were single parents, and 7% were something else.[[224]](#cite_note-habitation-228)

Since the 1980s, the average price of a single-family home has doubled every 10 years, going from $48,715 in 1980 to $424,844 in 2021. Since the average salary did not follow these increases, Quebec homes are 10 times more expensive than they were 40 years ago.[[334]](#cite_note-338) In 2022, the cities with the most severe housing shortages were [Granby](/wiki/Granby,_Quebec "Granby, Quebec"), with a vacancy rate of 0,1%, followed by [Marieville](/wiki/Marieville "Marieville") (0,1%), [Rimouski](/wiki/Rimouski "Rimouski") (0,2%), [Drummondville](/wiki/Drummondville "Drummondville") (0,2%) and [Rouyn-Noranda](/wiki/Rouyn-Noranda "Rouyn-Noranda") (0,3%).[[335]](#cite_note-339)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Quebec](/wiki/Culture_of_Quebec "Culture of Quebec")

Quebec has developed its own unique culture from its historic [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") roots. Its culture also symbolizes a distinct perspective: being a French-speaking nation surrounded by a bigger English-speaking culture.

The [Quartier Latin](/wiki/Quartier_Latin,_Montreal "Quartier Latin, Montreal") (English: Latin Quarter) of Montreal, and Vieux-Québec (English: [Old Quebec](/wiki/Old_Quebec "Old Quebec")) in Quebec City are two hubs of metropolitan cultural activity. Life in the cafés and "terrasses" (outdoor restaurant terraces) reveals a Latin influence in Quebec's culture, with the [théâtre Saint-Denis](/wiki/Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_Saint-Denis "Théâtre Saint-Denis") in Montreal and the [Capitole de Québec](/wiki/Capitole_de_Qu%C3%A9bec "Capitole de Québec") theatre in Quebec City being among the principal attractions.

A number of governmental and non-government organizations support cultural activity in Quebec. The [Conseil des arts et des lettres du Québec](/wiki/Conseil_des_arts_et_des_lettres_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Conseil des arts et des lettres du Québec") (CALQ) is an initiative of the [Ministry of Culture and Communications (Quebec)](/wiki/Ministry_of_Culture_and_Communications_(Quebec) "Ministry of Culture and Communications (Quebec)"). It supports creation, innovation, production, and international exhibits for all cultural fields of Quebec. The [Société de développement des entreprises culturelles](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_d%C3%A9veloppement_des_entreprises_culturelles "Société de développement des entreprises culturelles") (SODEC) works to promote and fund individuals working in the cultural industry. The [Prix du Québec](/wiki/Prix_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Prix du Québec") is an award given by the government to confer the highest distinction and honour to individuals demonstrating exceptional achievement in their respective cultural field. Other awards include the Athanase David Awards (Literature), [Félix Awards](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Awards "Félix Awards") (Music), [Gémeaux Awards](/wiki/G%C3%A9meaux_Awards "Gémeaux Awards") (Television and film), [Jutra Awards](/wiki/Jutra_Awards "Jutra Awards") (Cinema), Masques Awards (Theatre), [Olivier Guimond](/wiki/Olivier_Guimond "Olivier Guimond") Awards (Humour) and the Opus Awards (Concert music).

### Performing arts

Main articles: [Music of Quebec](/wiki/Music_of_Quebec "Music of Quebec"), [Dance in Quebec](/wiki/Dance_in_Quebec "Dance in Quebec"), and [Comedy in Quebec](/wiki/Comedy_in_Quebec "Comedy in Quebec")

Traditional music is imbued with many dances, such as the [jig](/wiki/Jig "Jig"), the [quadrille](/wiki/Quadrille "Quadrille"), the [reel](/wiki/Reel_(dance) "Reel (dance)") and [line dancing](/wiki/Line_dancing "Line dancing"). Traditional instruments include harmonica, [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), [spoons](/wiki/Spoon_(musical_instrument) "Spoon (musical instrument)"), [jaw harp](/wiki/Jaw_harp "Jaw harp") and [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion"). The [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") and the [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") of Quebec also have their own traditional music. Quebec's most popular artists of the last century include the singers [Félix Leclerc](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Leclerc "Félix Leclerc"), [Gilles Vigneault](/wiki/Gilles_Vigneault "Gilles Vigneault"), [Kate and Anna McGarrigle](/wiki/Kate_and_Anna_McGarrigle "Kate and Anna McGarrigle") and [Céline Dion](/wiki/C%C3%A9line_Dion "Céline Dion").[[336]](#cite_note-340) The [Association québécoise de l'industrie du disque, du spectacle et de la vidéo](/wiki/ADISQ "ADISQ") (ADISQ) was created in 1978 to promote the music industry in Quebec.[[337]](#cite_note-341) The [Orchestre symphonique de Québec](/wiki/Orchestre_symphonique_de_Qu%C3%A9bec "Orchestre symphonique de Québec") and the [Montreal Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Montreal_Symphony_Orchestra "Montreal Symphony Orchestra") are respectively associated with the Opéra de Québec and the Opéra de Montreal whose performances are presented at the [Grand Théâtre de Québec](/wiki/Grand_Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_Qu%C3%A9bec "Grand Théâtre de Québec") and at [Place des Arts](/wiki/Place_des_Arts "Place des Arts"). The *Ballets Jazz de Montreal*, the *Grands Ballets* and [La La La Human Steps](/wiki/La_La_La_Human_Steps "La La La Human Steps") are three important professional troupes of [contemporary dance](/wiki/Contemporary_dance "Contemporary dance").

Among the theatre troupes are the [Compagnie Jean-Duceppe](/wiki/Compagnie_Jean-Duceppe "Compagnie Jean-Duceppe"), the *Théâtre La Rubrique*, and the Théâtre Le Grenier. In addition to the network of cultural centres in Quebec,[[338]](#cite_note-342) the venues include the [Monument-National](/wiki/Monument-National "Monument-National") and the *Rideau Vert* (green curtain) Theatre in Montreal, and the *Trident* Theatre in [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"). The [National Theatre School of Canada](/wiki/National_Theatre_School_of_Canada "National Theatre School of Canada") and the [Conservatoire de musique et d'art dramatique du Québec](/wiki/Conservatoire_de_musique_et_d%27art_dramatique_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Conservatoire de musique et d'art dramatique du Québec") form the future players.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Dralion-Vienna.jpg/250px-Dralion-Vienna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dralion-Vienna.jpg)

The show *Dralion*, Cirque du Soleil, introduced in 2004

Several circus troupes were created in recent decades, the most important being the [Cirque du Soleil](/wiki/Cirque_du_Soleil "Cirque du Soleil").[[339]](#cite_note-343) Among these troops are contemporary, travelling and on-horseback circuses, such as [Les 7 Doigts de la Main](/w/index.php?title=Les_7_Doigts_de_la_Main&action=edit&redlink=1 "Les 7 Doigts de la Main (page does not exist)"), [Cirque Éloize](/wiki/Cirque_%C3%89loize "Cirque Éloize"), [Cavalia](/wiki/Cavalia "Cavalia"), *Kosmogonia*, *Saka* and Cirque *Akya*.[[340]](#cite_note-344) The [National Circus School](/wiki/%C3%89cole_nationale_de_cirque "École nationale de cirque") and the *École de cirque de Québec* were created to train future [Contemporary circus](/wiki/Contemporary_circus "Contemporary circus") artists. *[Tohu, la Cité des Arts du Cirque](/w/index.php?title=Tohu,_la_Cit%C3%A9_des_Arts_du_Cirque&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tohu, la Cité des Arts du Cirque (page does not exist)")* was founded in 2004 to disseminate the circus arts.[[341]](#cite_note-345)

Comedy is a vast [cultural sector](/wiki/Comedy_in_Quebec "Comedy in Quebec"). Quebec has created and is home to several different comedy festivals, including the [Just for Laughs](/wiki/Just_for_Laughs "Just for Laughs") festival in Montreal, as well as the Grand Rire festivals of Quebec, Gatineau and Sherbrooke.[[342]](#cite_note-346) The *Association des professionnels de l'industrie de l'humour* (APIH) is the main organization for the promotion and development of the cultural sector of humour in Quebec and the [National School of Humour](/w/index.php?title=National_School_of_Humour&action=edit&redlink=1 "National School of Humour (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89cole_nationale_de_l%27humour "fr:École nationale de l'humour")], created in 1988, trains future humorists in Quebec.

### Media

Main articles: [Cinema of Quebec](/wiki/Cinema_of_Quebec "Cinema of Quebec") and [Television in Quebec](/wiki/Television_in_Quebec "Television in Quebec")

The [Cinémathèque québécoise](/wiki/Cin%C3%A9math%C3%A8que_qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9coise "Cinémathèque québécoise") has a mandate to promote the film and television heritage of Quebec. The [National Film Board of Canada](/wiki/National_Film_Board_of_Canada "National Film Board of Canada") (NFB), a federal Crown corporation, provides for the same mission in Canada. The Association of Film and Television in Quebec (APFTQ) promotes independent production in film and television.[[343]](#cite_note-347) While the Association of Producers and Directors of Quebec (APDQ) represents the business of filmmaking and television, the Association of Community Radio Broadcasters of Quebec (ARCQ) (French acronym) represents the independent radio stations.[[344]](#cite_note-348) Several movie theatres across Quebec ensure the dissemination of Quebec cinema. With its cinematic installations, such as the *Cité du cinéma* and *Mel's* studios, the city of Montreal is home to the filming of various productions.[[345]](#cite_note-349) The state corporation [Télé-Québec](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9-Qu%C3%A9bec "Télé-Québec"), the federal Crown corporation [CBC](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation"), general and specialized private channels, networks, independent and community radio stations broadcast the various [Quebec téléromans](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9roman "Téléroman"), the national and regional news, and other programming.[[346]](#cite_note-350)[[347]](#cite_note-351) [Les Rendez-vous du cinéma québécois](/wiki/Les_Rendez-vous_du_cin%C3%A9ma_qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Les Rendez-vous du cinéma québécois") is a festival surrounding the ceremony of the [Jutra Awards](/wiki/Jutra_Award "Jutra Award") Night that rewards work and personalities of Quebec cinema.[[348]](#cite_note-352) The Artis and the [Gemini Awards](/wiki/Gemini_Awards "Gemini Awards") gala recognize the personalities of television and radio industry in Quebec and French Canada. The *Film Festival of the 3 Americas*, the *Festival of International Short Film*, the [World Film Festival](/wiki/Montreal_World_Film_Festival "Montreal World Film Festival") and the [Festival of New Cinema](/wiki/Festival_du_Nouveau_Cin%C3%A9ma "Festival du Nouveau Cinéma") are other annual events surrounding the film industry in Quebec.

In the realm of literature and international publishing, the *Québec Édition* group is a committee created by the National Association of Book Editors dedicated to the international influence of French-language publishings from Quebec and Canada.[[349]](#cite_note-353)

### Literature and folklore

Main articles: [Quebec literature](/wiki/Quebec_literature "Quebec literature") and [Folklore of Quebec](/wiki/Folklore_of_Quebec "Folklore of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/La_Chasse-galerie_%281906%29.jpg/250px-La_Chasse-galerie_%281906%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Chasse-galerie_(1906).jpg)

*La [chasse-galerie](/wiki/Chasse-galerie "Chasse-galerie")* (1906) by [Henri Julien](/wiki/Henri_Julien "Henri Julien"), showing a scene from a popular Quebec folk legend

Quebec's French-speaking populace has the second largest body of folktales in Canada (the first being [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada")).[[350]](#cite_note-354) When the early settlers arrived from France in the 17th century, they brought with them popular tales from their homeland, which were adapted to the local context. Many were passed on through generations by *raconteurs*, or storytellers.[[351]](#cite_note-355) Almost all of the stories native to Quebec were influenced by Christian [dogma](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma") and [superstitions](/wiki/Superstitions "Superstitions"). The [Devil](/wiki/Devil "Devil"), for instance, appears often as either a person, an animal or monster, or indirectly through Demonic acts.[[352]](#cite_note-Chiasson2-356) Various tales and stories are told through oral tradition, such as, among many others, the legends of the *[Bogeyman](/wiki/Bogeyman "Bogeyman")*, the *[Chasse-galerie](/wiki/Chasse-galerie "Chasse-galerie")*, the *Black Horse of Trois-Pistoles*, the *Complainte de Cadieux*, the *[Corriveau](/wiki/Marie-Josephte_Corriveau "Marie-Josephte Corriveau")*, the *dancing devil of Saint-Ambroise*, the *[Giant Beaupré](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Beaupr%C3%A9 "Édouard Beaupré")*, the *monsters of the lakes [Pohénégamook](/wiki/Lake_Pohenegamook "Lake Pohenegamook")* and *[Memphremagog](/wiki/Lake_Memphremagog "Lake Memphremagog")*, of *[Quebec Bridge](/wiki/Quebec_Bridge "Quebec Bridge")* (called the Devil's Bridge), the *[Rocher Percé](/wiki/Rocher_Perc%C3%A9 "Rocher Percé")* and of *Rose Latulipe*, for example.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDupont2008-357)

From New France, Quebec literature was first developed in the travel accounts of explorers. The *Moulin à paroles* traces the great texts that have shaped the history of Quebec. The first to write the history of Quebec, since its discovery, was the historian [François-Xavier Garneau](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Xavier_Garneau "François-Xavier Garneau"). Many [Quebec poets](/wiki/List_of_Quebec_writers "List of Quebec writers") and prominent authors marked their era and today remain anchored in the collective imagination, like, among others, Philippe Aubert de Gaspé, [Octave Crémazie](/wiki/Octave_Cr%C3%A9mazie "Octave Crémazie"), [Honoré Beaugrand](/wiki/Honor%C3%A9_Beaugrand "Honoré Beaugrand"), [Émile Nelligan](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Nelligan "Émile Nelligan"), [Lionel Groulx](/wiki/Lionel_Groulx "Lionel Groulx"), [Gabrielle Roy](/wiki/Gabrielle_Roy "Gabrielle Roy"), [Hubert Aquin](/wiki/Hubert_Aquin "Hubert Aquin"), [Michel Tremblay](/wiki/Michel_Tremblay "Michel Tremblay"), [Marie Laberge](/wiki/Marie_Laberge "Marie Laberge"), Fred Pellerin and [Gaston Miron](/wiki/Gaston_Miron "Gaston Miron"). The [regional novel](/wiki/American_Literary_Regionalism "American Literary Regionalism") from Quebec is called *Terroir* novel and is a literary tradition[[354]](#cite_note-358) specific to the province.

### Art and architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Quebec](/wiki/Architecture_of_Quebec "Architecture of Quebec")

See also: [List of art schools in Quebec](/wiki/List_of_art_schools_in_Quebec "List of art schools in Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Daudelin-Cavali%C3%A8re-Qu%C3%A9bec.JPG/250px-Daudelin-Cavali%C3%A8re-Qu%C3%A9bec.JPG)](/wiki/File:Daudelin-Cavali%C3%A8re-Qu%C3%A9bec.JPG)

*La Cavalière* by [Charles Daudelin](/wiki/Charles_Daudelin "Charles Daudelin"), 1963, installed in front of the pavilion Gérard Morisset of the Quebec National Museum of Fine Arts in [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City")

The art of Quebec has developed around the specific characteristics of its landscapes and cultural, historical, social and political representations. The development of Quebec masterpieces in painting, printmaking and sculpture is marked by the contribution of artists such as [Louis-Philippe Hébert](/wiki/Louis-Philippe_H%C3%A9bert "Louis-Philippe Hébert"), [Cornelius Krieghoff](/wiki/Cornelius_Krieghoff "Cornelius Krieghoff"), [Alfred Laliberté](/wiki/Alfred_Lalibert%C3%A9 "Alfred Laliberté"), [Marc-Aurèle Fortin](/wiki/Marc-Aur%C3%A8le_Fortin "Marc-Aurèle Fortin"), [Marc-Aurèle de Foy Suzor-Coté](/wiki/Marc-Aur%C3%A8le_de_Foy_Suzor-Cot%C3%A9 "Marc-Aurèle de Foy Suzor-Coté"), [Jean Paul Lemieux](/wiki/Jean_Paul_Lemieux "Jean Paul Lemieux"), [Clarence Gagnon](/wiki/Clarence_Gagnon "Clarence Gagnon"), Adrien Dufresne, [Alfred Pellan](/wiki/Alfred_Pellan "Alfred Pellan"), [Jean-Philippe Dallaire](/wiki/Jean-Philippe_Dallaire "Jean-Philippe Dallaire"), [Charles Daudelin](/wiki/Charles_Daudelin "Charles Daudelin"), [Arthur Villeneuve](/wiki/Arthur_Villeneuve "Arthur Villeneuve"), [Jean-Paul Riopelle](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Riopelle "Jean-Paul Riopelle"), [Paul-Émile Borduas](/wiki/Paul-%C3%89mile_Borduas "Paul-Émile Borduas") and [Marcelle Ferron](/wiki/Marcelle_Ferron "Marcelle Ferron").

The fine arts of Quebec are displayed at the [Quebec National Museum of Fine Arts](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_national_des_beaux-arts_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Musée national des beaux-arts du Québec"), the [Montreal Museum of Contemporary Art](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_d%27art_contemporain_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Musée d'art contemporain de Montréal"), the [Montreal Museum of Fine Arts](/wiki/Montreal_Museum_of_Fine_Arts "Montreal Museum of Fine Arts"), the Quebec *Salon des métiers d'art* and in many art galleries. The [Montreal School of Fine Arts](/wiki/%C3%89cole_des_Beaux-Arts_de_Montr%C3%A9al "École des Beaux-Arts de Montréal") forms the [painters, printmakers and sculptors of Quebec](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_artists "List of Canadian artists").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Maison_Routhier%2C_Qu%C3%A9bec.jpg/250px-Maison_Routhier%2C_Qu%C3%A9bec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maison_Routhier,_Qu%C3%A9bec.jpg)

*Maison Routhier* in [Sainte-Foy](/wiki/Sainte-Foy,_Quebec_City "Sainte-Foy, Quebec City"), a [Canadien](/wiki/Canadien "Canadien")-style house

Quebec's architecture is characterized by its unique [Canadien](/wiki/Canadien "Canadien")-style buildings as well as the juxtaposition of a variety of styles reflective of Quebec's history. When walking in any city or town, one can come across buildings with styles congruent to [Classical](/wiki/Classical_architecture "Classical architecture"), [Neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic"), [Roman](/wiki/Roman_architecture "Roman architecture"), [Neo-Renaissance](/wiki/Neo-Renaissance "Neo-Renaissance"), [Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture"), [Neo-Classical](/wiki/Neo-classical_architecture "Neo-classical architecture"), Québécois Neo-Classical, [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture"), [Second Empire](/wiki/Second_Empire_architecture "Second Empire architecture"), [Modern](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture"), [Post-modern](/wiki/Post-modern_architecture "Post-modern architecture") or [Skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscrapers "Skyscrapers").

Canadien-style houses and barns were developed by the first settlers of New France along the banks of the Saint Lawrence River. These buildings are rectangular one-storey structures with an extremely tall and steep roof, sometimes almost twice as tall as the house below. Canadien-style churches also developed and served as landmarks while traversing rural Quebec.

### Heritage

Main articles: [Quebec Cultural Heritage Directory](/wiki/Quebec_Cultural_Heritage_Directory "Quebec Cultural Heritage Directory") and [Bibliothèque et Archives nationales du Québec](/wiki/Biblioth%C3%A8que_et_Archives_nationales_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Bibliothèque et Archives nationales du Québec")

See also: [List of museums in Quebec](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Quebec "List of museums in Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Baie_Johan_Beetz_005.jpg/250px-Baie_Johan_Beetz_005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baie_Johan_Beetz_005.jpg)

[Johan Beetz](/wiki/Johan_Beetz "Johan Beetz") House,[[355]](#cite_note-Johan_Beetz_House,_heritage-359) a [Second Empire-inspired](/wiki/Second_Empire_style "Second Empire style") rural residence at [Baie-Johan-Beetz](/wiki/Baie-Johan-Beetz "Baie-Johan-Beetz"), [Minganie](/wiki/Minganie_Regional_County_Municipality "Minganie Regional County Municipality")

Several sites, houses and historical works reflect the cultural heritage of Quebec, such as the [Village Québécois d'Antan](/wiki/Village_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois_d%27Antan "Village Québécois d'Antan"), the historical village of [Val-Jalbert](/wiki/Val-Jalbert "Val-Jalbert"), the [Fort Chambly](/wiki/Fort_Chambly "Fort Chambly"), the national home of the Patriots, the Chicoutimi pulp mill (Pulperie de Chicoutimi), the [Lachine Canal](/wiki/Lachine_Canal "Lachine Canal") and the [Victoria Bridge](/wiki/Victoria_Bridge_(Montreal) "Victoria Bridge (Montreal)"). As of December 2011, there are 198 [National Historic Sites of Canada](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada_in_Quebec "List of National Historic Sites of Canada in Quebec") in Quebec.[[356]](#cite_note-Explore_Quebec,_Parc_Canada-360) These sites were designated as being of national historic significance.[[357]](#cite_note-361)

Various museums tell the cultural history of Quebec, like the [Museum of Civilization](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_la_civilisation "Musée de la civilisation"), the [Museum of French America](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_l%27Am%C3%A9rique_fran%C3%A7aise "Musée de l'Amérique française"), the [McCord Museum](/wiki/McCord_Museum "McCord Museum") or the Montreal Museum of Archaeology and History in [Pointe-à-Callière](/wiki/Pointe-%C3%A0-Calli%C3%A8re "Pointe-à-Callière"), displaying artifacts, paintings and other remains from the past of Quebec. Notable schools include the [Conservatoire de musique et d'art dramatique du Québec](/wiki/Conservatoire_de_musique_et_d%27art_dramatique_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Conservatoire de musique et d'art dramatique du Québec"), the [École nationale de théâtre du Canada](/wiki/National_Theatre_School_of_Canada "National Theatre School of Canada") and the [École nationale de cirque](/wiki/%C3%89cole_nationale_de_cirque "École nationale de cirque"). Notable public agencies to catalogue and further develop Quebec's culture include the [Bibliothèque et Archives nationales du Québec](/wiki/Biblioth%C3%A8que_et_Archives_nationales_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Bibliothèque et Archives nationales du Québec"), the [Conseil des arts et des lettres du Québec](/wiki/Conseil_des_arts_et_des_lettres_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Conseil des arts et des lettres du Québec") and [Télé-Québec](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9-Qu%C3%A9bec "Télé-Québec"). The *Association Quebecoise des Loisirs Folkloriques* is an organization committed to preserving and disseminating Quebec's folklore heritage.[[358]](#cite_note-362)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Cuisine of Quebec](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Quebec "Cuisine of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/La_Banquise_Poutine.jpg/250px-La_Banquise_Poutine.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Banquise_Poutine.jpg)

A classic [poutine](/wiki/Poutine "Poutine")

The traditional Quebecois cuisine descends from 16th-century [French cuisine](/wiki/French_cuisine "French cuisine"), the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") and a history of [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting"). Quebec's cuisine has also been influenced by learning from [First Nation](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"), by [English cuisine](/wiki/English_cuisine "English cuisine") and by [American cuisine](/wiki/American_cuisine "American cuisine"). Quebec is most famous for its [tourtière](/wiki/Tourti%C3%A8re "Tourtière"), [pâté chinois](/wiki/P%C3%A2t%C3%A9_chinois "Pâté chinois"), [poutine](/wiki/Poutine "Poutine"), and [St. Catherine's taffy](/wiki/St._Catherine%27s_taffy "St. Catherine's taffy") among others. "Le temps des sucres" is a period during springtime when many Quebecers go to the [sugar shack](/wiki/Sugar_shack "Sugar shack") (*cabane à sucre*) for a traditional meal.

Quebec is the world's biggest [maple syrup](/wiki/Maple_syrup "Maple syrup") producer.[[359]](#cite_note-bbc17-363) The province has a long history of producing maple syrup, and creating new [maple-derived products](/wiki/List_of_foods_made_from_maple "List of foods made from maple"). Other major food products include beer, wine (including [ice wine](/wiki/Ice_wine "Ice wine") and [ice cider](/wiki/Ice_cider "Ice cider")), and cheese.

### Sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Boston_vs._CH_au_Centre_Bell_007.jpg/250px-Boston_vs._CH_au_Centre_Bell_007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boston_vs._CH_au_Centre_Bell_007.jpg)

The [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") at the [Bell Centre](/wiki/Bell_Centre "Bell Centre")

Sports in Quebec constitutes an essential dimension of Quebec culture. [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") remains the national sport. This sport was played for the first time on March 3, 1875, in Montreal and has been promoted over the years by numerous achievements, including the centenary of the [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens").[[360]](#cite_note-364) Other major sports include [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") with the [Montreal Alouettes](/wiki/Montreal_Alouettes "Montreal Alouettes"), soccer with [Club de Foot Montréal](/wiki/Club_de_Foot_Montr%C3%A9al "Club de Foot Montréal"), the [Grand Prix du Canada](/wiki/Canadian_Grand_Prix "Canadian Grand Prix") Formula 1 racing with drivers such as [Gilles Villeneuve](/wiki/Gilles_Villeneuve "Gilles Villeneuve") and [Jacques Villeneuve](/wiki/Jacques_Villeneuve "Jacques Villeneuve"), and professional baseball with the former [Montreal Expos](/wiki/Montreal_Expos "Montreal Expos"). Quebec has hosted several major sporting events, including the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics"), the Fencing World Championships in 1967, track cycling in 1974, and the [Transat Québec-Saint-Malo](/wiki/Transat_Qu%C3%A9bec-Saint-Malo "Transat Québec-Saint-Malo") race created in 1984.

Quebec athletes have performed well at the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") over recent years. They won 12 of [Canada's 29 medals](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2018 Winter Olympics") at the most recent [Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang (2018)](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics"); they won 12 of the [27 Canadian medals](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2014 Winter Olympics") in [Sochi (2014)](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics"); and 9 of the [26 Canadian medals](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2010 Winter Olympics") in [Vancouver (2010)](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics").[[361]](#cite_note-365)

### Holidays and symbols

Main article: [Symbols of Quebec](/wiki/Symbols_of_Quebec "Symbols of Quebec")

[St-Jean-Baptiste Day](/wiki/St-Jean-Baptiste_Day "St-Jean-Baptiste Day") is one of Quebec's biggest holidays. In 1977, the [Quebec Parliament](/wiki/Quebec_Parliament "Quebec Parliament") declared June 24, the day of *La Saint-Jean-Baptiste*, to be [Quebec's National Holiday](/wiki/Quebec%27s_National_Holiday "Quebec's National Holiday"). *La Saint-Jean-Baptiste*, or *La St-Jean*, honours [French Canada](/wiki/French_Canada "French Canada")'s patron saint, [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"). On this day, the song "[Gens du pays](/wiki/Gens_du_pays "Gens du pays")", by [Gilles Vigneault](/wiki/Gilles_Vigneault "Gilles Vigneault"), is often heard. The song *[À la claire fontaine](/wiki/%C3%80_la_claire_fontaine "À la claire fontaine")*[[362]](#cite_note-366) was the anthem of the [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), [Patriots](/wiki/Patriote_movement "Patriote movement") and [French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian"), then replaced by *[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")*, but "Gens du pays" is preferred by many Quebecers to be the national anthem of Quebec.

[National Patriots' Day](/wiki/National_Patriots%27_Day "National Patriots' Day"), a statutory holiday in Quebec, is also a unique public holiday, which honours the [patriotes](/wiki/Patriote_movement "Patriote movement") with displays of the [patriote flag](/wiki/Patriote_flag "Patriote flag"), music, public speeches, and ceremonies.[[363]](#cite_note-law2-367) *[Le Vieux de '37](/wiki/Le_Vieux_de_%2737 "Le Vieux de '37")* ("The Old Man of '37"), an illustration by [Henri Julien](/wiki/Henri_Julien "Henri Julien") that depicts a patriot of this rebellion,[[364]](#cite_note-368) is sometimes added at the centre of [Patriote flags](/wiki/Patriote_flags "Patriote flags"). [Moving Day](/wiki/Moving_Day_(Quebec) "Moving Day (Quebec)") is a tradition where leases terminate on July 1. This creates a [social phenomenon](/wiki/Social_phenomenon "Social phenomenon") where everyone seems to be moving out at the same time.[[365]](#cite_note-369)

Other distinct holiday traditions include the [Réveillon](/wiki/R%C3%A9veillon "Réveillon"), a giant feast and party which takes place during [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve") and [New Year's Eve](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Eve "New Year's Eve") and goes on until midnight. Traditional dishes like [tourtière](/wiki/Tourti%C3%A8re "Tourtière") or [cipâte](/wiki/Sea-pie "Sea-pie") are offered, and [rigaudon](/wiki/Rigaudon "Rigaudon"), [spoon](/wiki/Spoon_(musical_instrument) "Spoon (musical instrument)") or [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") may be played.[[366]](#cite_note-370) Finally, [April Fools' Day](/wiki/April_Fools%27_Day "April Fools' Day") is called *Poisson d'Avril* ("April's Fish") because while pulling pranks is still important, there is another major tradition: sticking fish-shaped paper cutouts to people's backs without them noticing.[[367]](#cite_note-371)

In 1939, the [government of Quebec](/wiki/Government_of_Quebec "Government of Quebec") unilaterally ratified its [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Quebec "Coat of arms of Quebec") to reflect [Quebec's political history](/wiki/History_of_Quebec "History of Quebec"): French rule (gold lily on blue background), followed by British rule (lion on red background), followed by Canadian rule (maple leaves).[[368]](#cite_note-372) [Je me souviens](/wiki/Je_me_souviens "Je me souviens") ("I remember") is an official part of the coat of arms and has been the official licence plate motto since 1978, replacing the previous motto: *La belle province* ("the beautiful province"), still used as a nickname for the province. The [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis"), one of Quebec's most common symbols, is an ancient symbol of the [French monarchy](/wiki/French_monarchy "French monarchy"). Finally, the [Great Seal of Quebec](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_Quebec "Great Seal of Quebec") is used to authenticate documents issued by the government of Quebec.

The first members of the [Saint-Jean-Baptiste Society](/wiki/Saint-Jean-Baptiste_Society "Saint-Jean-Baptiste Society") created the *Carillon Sacré-Coeur* flag, which consisted of a white cross on an azure background with white fleur-de-lis in each corner and a [Sacred Heart](/wiki/Sacred_Heart#Sacred_Heart_imagery "Sacred Heart") surrounded by [maple leaves](/wiki/Maple_leaf "Maple leaf") in the centre; it was based on the French merchant flag flown by Champlain and the [Flag of Carillon](/wiki/Flag_of_Carillon "Flag of Carillon"). The *Carillon Sacré-Coeur* and French merchant flag went on to be the major inspirations for creating Quebec's current flag in 1903, called the *[Fleurdelisé](/wiki/Fleurdelis%C3%A9 "Fleurdelisé")*. The *Fleurdelisé* replaced the [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") on Quebec's [Parliament Building](/wiki/Parliament_Building_(Quebec) "Parliament Building (Quebec)") on January 21, 1948.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Iris_versicolor.jpg/250px-Iris_versicolor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iris_versicolor.jpg)

*[Iris versicolor](/wiki/Iris_versicolor "Iris versicolor")* – [floral emblem of Quebec](#Emblems_of_Quebec)

Three new official emblems in were adopted in the late 20th century: the [Snowy owl](/wiki/Snowy_owl "Snowy owl") in 1987 to symbolize the whiteness of Quebec's semi-northern climate, the [Yellow birch](/wiki/Betula_alleghaniensis "Betula alleghaniensis") in 1993 for the variety of its uses and by its commercial value, and the [Iris versicolor](/wiki/Iris_versicolor "Iris versicolor") in 1999 to illustrate the cultural diversity of Quebec and the importance of water and wetlands for the balance of nature.[[369]](#cite_note-Qsymbols2-373)[[370]](#cite_note-Emblems_of_Quebec-374)

## Quebec's diaspora

Main article: [Quebec diaspora](/wiki/Quebec_diaspora "Quebec diaspora")

The earliest immigrants to the [Canadian prairies](/wiki/Canadian_prairies "Canadian prairies") were [French Canadians](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") from Quebec. Many [Franco-Albertans](/wiki/Franco-Albertans "Franco-Albertans"), [Fransaskois](/wiki/Fransaskois "Fransaskois") and [Franco-Manitobans](/wiki/Franco-Manitobans "Franco-Manitobans") are descended from them.

From the mid-1800s to the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), Quebec experienced the [Grande Hémorragie](/wiki/Grande_H%C3%A9morragie "Grande Hémorragie") ("Great Hemorrhaging"), a massive emigration of 900,000 people from Quebec to [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England").[[371]](#cite_note-375) French Canadians often established themselves in [Little Canadas](/wiki/Little_Canadas "Little Canadas") in many industrial New England centres. Of the 900,000 Québécois who emigrated, about half returned.[[372]](#cite_note-376) Most of the descendants of those who stayed are now [assimilated](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation"), though a few [Franco-Americans](/wiki/Franco-Americans "Franco-Americans") remain, speaking [New England French](/wiki/New_England_French "New England French").

Some tried to slow the Grande Hémorragie by redirecting people north, which resulted in the founding of many regions in Quebec (ex. [Saguenay-Lac-St-Jean](/wiki/Saguenay-Lac-St-Jean "Saguenay-Lac-St-Jean"), [Val-d'Or](/wiki/Val-d%27Or "Val-d'Or")) but also in [Northeastern Ontario](/wiki/Northeastern_Ontario "Northeastern Ontario"). The northeastern [Franco-Ontarians](/wiki/Franco-Ontarian "Franco-Ontarian") of today, who live in [Timmins](/wiki/Timmins "Timmins"), [Hearst](/wiki/Hearst,_Ontario "Hearst, Ontario"), [Moosonee](/wiki/Moosonee "Moosonee") and [Sault Sainte Marie](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Ontario "Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario"), among others, are the descendants of emigrants from Quebec who worked in the mines of the area.[[373]](#cite_note-377)

In recent times, [snowbirds](/wiki/Snowbird_(person) "Snowbird (person)") often migrate to southern [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") during the winter, resulting in the emergence of temporary "Québécois regions," such as in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Florida "Hollywood, Florida").[[374]](#cite_note-378)
